etymology: Old French abaissier, from Latin ad- ('to') + bassus ('low')
exampleSentence: After being overthrown and abased, the deposed leader offered to bow down to his conqueror.

etymology: Old French abatre, from Latin ad- + battuere ('to beat down')
exampleSentence: The rain poured down for a while, then abated.

definition: To give up a position, usually one of leadership
etymology: Latin abdicare, from ab- ('away') + dicare ('to proclaim')
exampleSentence: When he realized that the revolutionaries would surely win, the king abdicated his throne.

etymology: Latin abducere, from ab- ('away') + ducere ('to lead')
exampleSentence: The evildoers abducted the fairy princess from her happy home.

definition: Something that differs from the norm
etymology: Latin aberratio, from aberrare ('to wander away'), from ab- + errare ('to stray')
exampleSentence: In 1918, the Boston Red Sox won the World Series, but the success turned out to be an aberration.

etymology: Old French abeter, from a- + beter ('to bait'), of Germanic origin
exampleSentence: The spy succeeded only because he had a friend on the inside to abet him.

etymology: Latin abhorrere, from ab- ('away from') + horrere ('to shudder')
exampleSentence: Because he always wound up kicking himself in the head when he tried to play soccer, Oswald began to abhor the sport.

etymology: Old English abidan, from a- (intensive prefix) + bidan ('to wait, remain')

senses.exampleSentence: Though he did not agree with the decision, Chuck decided to abide by it.
Despite the beating they've taken from the weather throughout the millennia, the mountains abide.

etymology: Latin abjectus, past participle of abicere ('to throw away'), from ab- + jacere ('to throw')
exampleSentence: After losing all her money, falling into a puddle, and breaking her ankle, Eloise was abject.

etymology: Latin abjurare, from ab- ('away') + jurare ('to swear')
exampleSentence: To prove his honesty, the President abjured the evil policies of his wicked predecessor.

etymology: Latin abnegatio, from abnegare ('to refuse'), from ab- + negare ('to deny')
exampleSentence: The holy man slept on the floor, took only cold showers, and generally followed other practices of abnegation.

definition: To give up on a half-finished project or effort
etymology: Latin abortare, from aboriri ('to miscarry'), from ab- + oriri ('to arise, be born')
exampleSentence: After they ran out of food, the men attempting to jump rope around the world had to abort and go home.

etymology: Old French abregier, from Latin abbreviare ('to shorten'), from ad- + brevis ('short')

senses.exampleSentence: The publisher thought the dictionary was too long and abridged it.
Moby-Dick is such a long book that even the abridged version is longer than most normal books.

etymology: Latin abrogare, from ab- + rogare ('to propose a law, ask')
exampleSentence: The Bill of Rights assures that the government cannot abrogate our right to a free press.

etymology: Latin abscondere, from abs- ('away') + condere ('to hide, store')
exampleSentence: In the confusion, the super-spy absconded into the night with the secret plans.

etymology: Latin absolutio, from absolvere ('to set free'), from ab- + solvere ('to loosen')
exampleSentence: Once all the facts were known, the jury gave Angela absolution by giving a verdict of not guilty.

definition: To freely choose not to commit an action
etymology: Latin abstinere, from abs- ('from') + tenere ('to hold')
exampleSentence: Everyone demanded that Angus put on the kilt, but he did not want to do it and abstained.

etymology: Latin abstrusus, past participle of abstrudere ('to conceal'), from abs- + trudere ('to push')
exampleSentence: Everyone else in the class understood geometry easily, but John found the subject abstruse.

etymology: Latin accedere, from ad- ('to') + cedere ('to go, yield')
exampleSentence: When the class asked the teacher whether they could play baseball instead of learn grammar, he acceded to their request.

etymology: Medieval Latin accentuare, from Latin accentus ('accent'), from ad- + cantus ('song')
exampleSentence: Psychologists agree that those people who are happiest accentuate the positive in life.

etymology: Latin accessibilis, from accedere ('to approach'), from ad- + cedere ('to go')
exampleSentence: After studying with SparkNotes and getting a great score on the SAT, Marlena realized her goal of getting into an Ivy-League college was accessible.

etymology: Latin acclamare, from ad- + clamare ('to shout')
exampleSentence: Greg's excellent poem won the acclaim of his friends.

etymology: French accolade, from accoler ('to embrace'), from Latin ad- + collum ('neck')
exampleSentence: Everyone offered accolades to Sam after he won the Nobel Prize.

etymology: Latin accommodare, from ad- + commodus ('fitting'), from com- + modus ('measure')
exampleSentence: Though the apartment was not big enough for three people, Arnold, Mark, and Zebulon were all friends and were accommodating to each other.

etymology: Old French acorder, from Latin accordare, from ad- + cor ('heart')
exampleSentence: After much negotiating, England and Iceland finally came to a mutually beneficial accord about fishing rights.

etymology: French accoster, from Italian accostare, from Latin ad- + costa ('rib, side')
exampleSentence: Though Antoinette was normally quite calm, when the waiter spilled soup on her for the fourth time she stood up and accosted the man.

definition: Slow growth in size or amount
etymology: Latin accretio, from accrescere ('to grow'), from ad- + crescere ('to grow')
exampleSentence: Stalactites are formed by the accretion of minerals from the roofs of caves.

definition: Biting, bitter in tone or taste
etymology: Latin acerbus ('bitter, harsh'), related to acer ('sharp')
exampleSentence: Jill became extremely acerbic and began to cruelly make fun of all her friends.

etymology: Latin acquiescere, from ad- + quiescere ('to be quiet'), from quies ('quiet')
exampleSentence: Though Mr. Correlli wanted to stay outside and work in his garage, when his wife told him to come in to dinner, he acquiesced.

etymology: Latin acrimonia, from acer ('sharp, bitter') + -monia (noun suffix)
exampleSentence: Though they vowed that no girl would ever come between them, Biff and Trevor could not keep acrimony from overwhelming their friendship.

etymology: Latin acumen ('sharpness'), from acuere ('to sharpen'), from acus ('needle')
exampleSentence: Because of his mathematical acumen, Larry was able to figure out in minutes problems that took other students hours.

etymology: Latin acutus, past participle of acuere ('to sharpen'), from acus ('needle')

senses.exampleSentence: Arnold could not walk because the pain in his foot was so acute.
Because she was so acute, Libby instantly figured out how the magician pulled off his trick.

etymology: Latin adamans, from Greek adamas ('unconquerable'), from a- ('not') + daman ('to tame')
exampleSentence: Though public pressure was intense, the President remained adamant about his proposal.

etymology: Latin adeptus, past participle of adipisci ('to attain'), from ad- + apisci ('to reach')
exampleSentence: Tarzan was adept at jumping from tree to tree like a monkey.

etymology: Latin adhaerere, from ad- + haerere ('to stick')

senses.exampleSentence: We adhered the poster to the wall with tape.
He adhered to the dictates of his religion without question.

etymology: Old French admonester, from Latin admonere ('to advise'), from ad- + monere ('to warn')
exampleSentence: Joe's mother admonished him not to ruin his appetite by eating cookies before dinner.

etymology: Latin adornare, from ad- + ornare ('to furnish, decorate')
exampleSentence: We adorned the tree with ornaments.

etymology: French adroit, from à droit ('according to right'), from Latin directus ('straight')
exampleSentence: The adroit thief could pick someone's pocket without attracting notice.

etymology: Latin adulatio, from adulari ('to fawn on'), possibly from cauda ('tail') — as a dog wagging its tail
exampleSentence: Though the book was pretty good, Marcy did not believe it deserved the adulation it received.

definition: To sketch out in a vague way
etymology: Latin adumbrare ('to sketch'), from ad- + umbra ('shadow')
exampleSentence: The coach adumbrated a game plan, but none of the players knew precisely what to do.

etymology: Latin adversus ('turned against'), from advertere, from ad- + vertere ('to turn')
exampleSentence: Because of adverse conditions, the hikers decided to give up trying to climb the mountain.

etymology: Latin advocare ('to call to one's aid'), from ad- + vocare ('to call')

senses.definition: To argue in favor of something
senses.exampleSentence: Arnold advocated turning left at the stop sign, even though everyone else thought we should turn right.

senses.definition: A person who argues in favor of something
senses.exampleSentence: In addition to wanting to turn left at every stop sign, Arnold was also a great advocate of increasing national defense spending.

etymology: Latin aerius, from Greek aerios, from aer ('air')
exampleSentence: We watched as the fighter planes conducted aerial maneuvers.

definition: Artistic, related to the appreciation of beauty
etymology: Greek aisthetikos ('of sense perception'), from aisthanesthai ('to perceive')
exampleSentence: We hired Susan as our interior decorator because she has such a fine aesthetic sense.

etymology: Latin affabilis, from affari ('to speak to'), from ad- + fari ('to speak')
exampleSentence: People like to be around George because he is so affable and good-natured.

etymology: Latin affinitas ('relationship by marriage'), from affinis ('bordering on'), from ad- + finis ('border')
exampleSentence: Jerry didn't know why, but he felt an incredible affinity for Kramer the first time they met.

etymology: Latin affluens, from affluere ('to flow toward'), from ad- + fluere ('to flow')
exampleSentence: Mrs. Grebelski was affluent, owning a huge house, three cars, and an island near Maine.

etymology: Old French afronter ('to slap in the face'), from Latin ad- + frons ('forehead, face')
exampleSentence: Bernardo was very touchy, and took any slight as an affront to his honor.

etymology: French agrandir, from a- + grandir ('to enlarge'), from Latin grandire, from grandis ('great')
exampleSentence: Joseph always dropped the names of the famous people his father knew as a way to aggrandize his personal stature.

etymology: Latin aggregatus, from aggregare ('to add to a flock'), from ad- + grex ('flock')

senses.exampleSentence: The three branches of the U.S. Government form an aggregate much more powerful than its individual parts.
The dictator tried to aggregate as many people into his army as he possibly could.

etymology: Old French agrever ('to make heavier'), from Latin aggravare, from ad- + gravis ('heavy')
exampleSentence: The foreman mercilessly overworked his aggrieved employees.

etymology: Latin agilis, from agere ('to do, drive')
exampleSentence: The dogs were too slow to catch the agile rabbit.

definition: Believing that the existence of God cannot be proven or disproven
etymology: Greek agnostos ('unknown'), from a- ('not') + gnostos ('known'), coined by T.H. Huxley in 1869
exampleSentence: Joey's parents are very religious, but he is agnostic.

etymology: Latin agricultura, from ager ('field') + cultura ('cultivation'), from colere ('to cultivate')
exampleSentence: It was a huge step in civilization when tribes left hunting and gathering and began to develop more sustainable methods of obtaining food, such as agriculture.

definition: A passageway between rows of seats
etymology: Old French ele ('wing of a building'), from Latin ala ('wing'); spelling influenced by isle
exampleSentence: Once we got inside the stadium we walked down the aisle to our seats.

etymology: Latin alacritas, from alacer ('brisk, lively')
exampleSentence: For some reason, Chuck loved to help his mother whenever he could, so when his mother asked him to set the table he did so with alacrity.

etymology: Latin alias ('otherwise, at another time'), from alius ('other')
exampleSentence: He snuck past the guards by using an alias and fake ID.

etymology: Old English alecgan, from a- (intensive) + lecgan ('to lay')
exampleSentence: The chairman of the Federal Reserve gave a speech to try to allay investors' fears about an economic downturn.

etymology: Old French alegier, from Latin allegare ('to send on a mission'), from ad- + legare ('to depute')
exampleSentence: The policeman had alleged that Marshall committed the crime, but after the investigation turned up no evidence, Marshall was set free.

etymology: Latin alleviare, from ad- + levis ('light'), meaning to lighten a burden
exampleSentence: This drug will alleviate the symptoms of the terrible disease, but only for a while.

etymology: Medieval Latin allocare, from Latin ad- + locare ('to place'), from locus ('place')
exampleSentence: The Mayor allocated 30 percent of the funds for improving the town's schools.

etymology: Dutch te loef ('to windward'), nautical term meaning to keep a distance; loof relates to 'luff'
exampleSentence: The scientist could sometimes seem aloof, as if he didn't care about his friends or family, but really he was just thinking about quantum mechanics.

etymology: Latin altercatio, from altercari ('to dispute'), from alter ('other')
exampleSentence: Jason and Lionel blamed one another for the car accident, leading to an altercation.

etymology: Medieval Latin amalgamare, from amalgama ('alloy of mercury'), possibly from Greek malagma ('softening')
exampleSentence: Because of his great charisma, the presidential candidate was able to amalgamate all democrats and republicans under his banner.

etymology: Latin ambiguus, from ambigere ('to wander around'), from ambi- ('both ways') + agere ('to drive')
exampleSentence: Some people think Caesar married Cleopatra for her power, others believe he was charmed by her beauty. His actual reasons are ambiguous.

etymology: Latin ambi- ('both') + valentia ('strength'), coined in early 20th-century psychology
exampleSentence: My feelings about Calvin are ambivalent because on one hand he is a loyal friend, but on the other, he is a cruel and vicious thief.

etymology: French améliorer, from Old French meilleur ('better'), from Latin meliorare, from melior ('better')
exampleSentence: The tense situation was ameliorated when Sam proposed a solution everyone could agree upon.

etymology: Anglo-French amenable, from Old French amener ('to lead to'), from a- + mener ('to lead'), from Latin minare ('to drive')
exampleSentence: Our father was amenable when we asked him to drive us to the farm so we could go apple picking.

etymology: Latin amenitas ('pleasantness'), from amoenus ('pleasant')
exampleSentence: Bill Gates's house is stocked with so many amenities, he never has to do anything for himself.

etymology: Old French amiable, from Latin amicabilis ('friendly'), from amicus ('friend')
exampleSentence: An amiable fellow, Harry got along with just about everyone.

etymology: Late Latin amicabilis, from Latin amicus ('friend'), from amare ('to love')
exampleSentence: Claudia and Jimmy got divorced, but amicably and without hard feelings.

etymology: Latin amorosus, from amor ('love'), from amare ('to love')
exampleSentence: Whenever Albert saw Mariah wear her slinky red dress, he began to feel quite amorous.

etymology: Greek amorphos, from a- ('without') + morphe ('form, shape')
exampleSentence: The effort was doomed from the start, because the reasons behind it were so amorphous and hard to pin down.

definition: Being out of correct chronological order
etymology: Greek anachronismos, from ana- ('back') + chronos ('time')
exampleSentence: In this book you're writing, you say that the Pyramids were built after the Titanic sank, which is anachronistic.

etymology: Greek analgesia ('absence of pain'), from an- ('without') + algos ('pain')
exampleSentence: Put this analgesic on the wound so that the poor man at least feels a little better.

definition: Similar to, so that an analogy can be drawn
etymology: Greek analogos ('proportionate'), from ana- ('according to') + logos ('ratio, proportion')
exampleSentence: Though they are unrelated genetically, the bone structure of whales and fish is quite analogous.

definition: One who wants to eliminate all government
etymology: Greek anarchos ('without a ruler'), from an- ('without') + archos ('ruler')
exampleSentence: An anarchist, Carmine wanted to dissolve every government everywhere.

etymology: Greek anathema ('thing devoted to evil'), from anatithenai ('to set up'), from ana- + tithenai ('to place')
exampleSentence: I never want to see that murderer. He is an anathema to me.

etymology: Greek anekdotos ('unpublished'), from an- ('not') + ekdotos ('published'), from ekdidonai ('to publish')
exampleSentence: After dinner, Marlon told an anecdote about the time he got his nose stuck in a toaster.

etymology: Greek anaisthesia ('lack of sensation'), from an- ('without') + aisthesis ('sensation')
exampleSentence: When the nerves in his spine were damaged, Mr. Hollins suffered anesthesia in his legs.

etymology: Old French angoisse, from Latin angustia ('tightness, distress'), from angustus ('narrow')
exampleSentence: Angelos suffered terrible anguish when he learned that Buffy had died.

etymology: Latin animatus, past participle of animare ('to give life to'), from anima ('breath, soul')
exampleSentence: When he begins to talk about drama, which is his true passion, he becomes very animated.

etymology: Latin annexare, from ad- + nectere ('to bind')

senses.exampleSentence: After defeating them in battle, the Russians annexed Poland.

senses.definition: A room attached to a larger room or space
senses.exampleSentence: He likes to do his studying in a little annex attached to the main reading room in the library.

etymology: Old French annuller, from Latin annullare, from ad- + nullus ('none')
exampleSentence: After seeing its unforeseen and catastrophic effects, Congress sought to annul the law.

definition: Something that does not fit into the normal order
etymology: Greek anomalia ('unevenness'), from anomalos ('uneven'), from an- ('not') + homalos ('even')
exampleSentence: "That rip in the spacetime continuum is certainly a spatial anomaly," said Spock to Captain Kirk.

etymology: Greek anonymos, from an- ('without') + onyma ('name')
exampleSentence: Mary received a love poem from an anonymous admirer.

etymology: Greek antagonisma, from antagonizesthai ('to contend against'), from anti- + agonizesthai ('to contend')
exampleSentence: Superman and Bizarro Superman shared a mutual antagonism, and often fought.

etymology: Latin antecedere ('to go before'), from ante- ('before') + cedere ('to go')
exampleSentence: The great tradition of Western culture had its antecedent in the culture of Ancient Greece.

etymology: Latin ante ('before') + diluvium ('flood'), referring to the Biblical flood — before Noah's flood
exampleSentence: The antediluvian man still believed that Eisenhower was president of the United States and that hot dogs cost a nickel.

definition: A selected collection of writings, songs, etc.
etymology: Greek anthologia ('flower gathering'), from anthos ('flower') + legein ('to gather')
exampleSentence: The new anthology of Bob Dylan songs contains all his greatest hits and a few songs you might never have heard before.

etymology: Greek antipatheia, from anti- ('against') + pathos ('feeling')
exampleSentence: I know you love me, but because you are a liar and a thief, I feel nothing but antipathy for you.

etymology: Latin antiquatus, past participle of antiquare ('to make old'), from antiquus ('ancient')
exampleSentence: That antiquated car has none of the features, like power windows and steering, that make modern cars so great.

etymology: Greek anti- ('against') + septikos ('putrefying'), from sepein ('to make rotten')
exampleSentence: The antiseptic hospital was very bare, but its cleanliness helped to keep patients healthy.

etymology: Greek antithesis ('opposition'), from anti- ('against') + tithenai ('to place')
exampleSentence: Your values, which hold war and violence in the highest esteem, are the antithesis of my pacifist beliefs.

etymology: Latin anxietas, from anxius ('troubled'), from angere ('to choke, distress')
exampleSentence: When he heard about the car crash, he felt anxiety because he knew that his girlfriend had been driving on that road.

etymology: Greek apathes ('without feeling'), from a- ('without') + pathos ('feeling')
exampleSentence: Uninterested in politics, Bruno was apathetic about whether he lived under a capitalist or communist regime.

etymology: Greek apokryphos ('hidden away'), from apo- ('away') + kryptein ('to hide')
exampleSentence: Because I am standing before you, it seems obvious that the stories circulating about my demise were apocryphal.

etymology: Old French apallir ('to grow pale'), from a- + pallir ('to be pale'), from Latin pallere
exampleSentence: The judge found the murderer's crimes and lack of remorse appalling.

etymology: Old French apaisier, from a- + pais ('peace'), from Latin pax
exampleSentence: When the child cries, the mother gives him candy to appease him.

definition: To assess the worth or value of
etymology: Old French aprisier, from a- + prisier ('to prize'), from Latin pretiare ('to value')
exampleSentence: A realtor will come over tonight to appraise our house.

etymology: Latin apprehendere ('to seize'), from ad- + prehendere ('to grasp')

senses.exampleSentence: The criminal was apprehended at the scene.
The student has trouble apprehending concepts in math and science.

etymology: Latin approbatio, from approbare ('to approve'), from ad- + probare ('to prove, test')
exampleSentence: The crowd welcomed the heroes with approbation.

etymology: Latin appropriare ('to make one's own'), from ad- + proprius ('one's own')
exampleSentence: The government appropriated the farmer's land without justification.

etymology: Latin aquaticus, from aqua ('water')
exampleSentence: The marine biologist studies starfish and other aquatic creatures.

etymology: Latin arabilis, from arare ('to plow')
exampleSentence: The farmer purchased a plot of arable land on which he will grow corn and sprouts.

definition: One who can resolve a dispute, make a decision
etymology: Latin arbiter ('witness, judge'), possibly from ar- ('to') + baetere ('to go')
exampleSentence: The divorce court judge will serve as the arbiter between the estranged husband and wife.

definition: Based on factors that appear random
etymology: Latin arbitrarius, from arbiter ('judge') — decisions made at a judge's discretion, not by rule
exampleSentence: The boy's decision to choose one college over another seems arbitrary.

definition: The process or act of resolving a dispute
etymology: Latin arbitratio, from arbitrari ('to give judgment'), from arbiter ('judge')
exampleSentence: The employee sought official arbitration when he could not resolve a disagreement with his supervisor.

etymology: Latin arboreus, from arbor ('tree')
exampleSentence: Leaves, roots, and bark are a few arboreal traits.

definition: Obscure, secret, known only by a few
etymology: Latin arcanus ('secret, hidden'), from arca ('chest, box')
exampleSentence: The professor is an expert in arcane Lithuanian literature.

definition: Of or relating to an earlier period in time, outdated
etymology: Greek archaikos ('old-fashioned'), from archaios ('ancient'), from arche ('beginning')
exampleSentence: In a few select regions of Western Mongolia, an archaic Chinese dialect is still spoken.

definition: The most representative or typical example of something
etymology: Greek archetupos ('original pattern'), from arche ('beginning') + tupos ('type, model')
exampleSentence: Some believe George Washington, with his flowing white hair and commanding stature, was the archetypal politician.

etymology: Latin ardor ('heat, flame'), from ardere ('to burn')
exampleSentence: The soldiers conveyed their ardor with impassioned battle cries.

etymology: Latin aridus, from arere ('to be dry')
exampleSentence: Little other than palm trees and cacti grow successfully in arid environments.

etymology: Latin arrogare ('to claim for oneself'), from ad- + rogare ('to ask')
exampleSentence: The king arrogated the right to order executions to himself exclusively.

definition: A remaining piece from an extinct culture or place
etymology: Latin arte ('by skill') + factum ('thing made'), from facere ('to make')
exampleSentence: The scientists spent all day searching the cave for artifacts from the ancient Mayan civilization.

etymology: French artisan, from Italian artigiano, from Latin artitus ('skilled in the arts'), from ars ('art')
exampleSentence: The artisan uses wood to make walking sticks.

etymology: Old French acertainer, from a- + certain ('certain'), from Latin certus ('sure')
exampleSentence: With a bit of research, the student ascertained that some plants can live for weeks without water.

definition: Practicing restraint as a means of self-discipline, usually religious
etymology: Greek asketes ('one who exercises'), from askein ('to exercise, train')
exampleSentence: The priest lives an ascetic life devoid of television, savory foods, and other pleasures.

etymology: Latin ascribere, from ad- + scribere ('to write') — to write something to someone's account
exampleSentence: Some ascribe the invention of fireworks and dynamite to the Chinese.

etymology: Latin aspersio ('a sprinkling'), from aspergere ('to sprinkle'), from ad- + spargere — as in sprinkling someone with mud
exampleSentence: The rival politicians repeatedly cast aspersions on each other's integrity.

etymology: Latin aspirare ('to breathe upon, aim at'), from ad- + spirare ('to breathe')
exampleSentence: The young poet aspires to publish a book of verse someday.

etymology: Old French assaillir, from Latin assalire, from ad- + salire ('to leap')
exampleSentence: At dawn, the war planes assailed the boats in the harbor.

etymology: Old French assesser, from Latin assidere ('to sit beside'), from ad- + sedere ('to sit') — as a judge sitting beside
exampleSentence: A crew arrived to assess the damage after the crash.

etymology: Latin assiduus ('constant, persistent'), from assidere ('to sit beside'), from ad- + sedere ('to sit')
exampleSentence: The construction workers erected the skyscraper during two years of assiduous labor.

etymology: Old French assouagier, from Latin ad- + suavis ('sweet, agreeable')
exampleSentence: The mother held the baby to assuage its fears.

etymology: Latin astutus, from astus ('craft, cunning')
exampleSentence: Much of Roger's success in politics results from his ability to provide astute answers to reporters' questions.

etymology: Latin asylum, from Greek asylon ('place of refuge'), from a- ('without') + syle ('right of seizure')

senses.definition: A place of refuge, protection, a sanctuary
senses.exampleSentence: For Thoreau, the forest served as an asylum from the pressures of urban life.

senses.definition: An institution in which the insane are kept
senses.exampleSentence: Once diagnosed by a certified psychiatrist, the man was put in an asylum.

etymology: Middle English at on ('in agreement'), from at + one — literally to become 'at one' with someone you wronged
exampleSentence: The man atoned for forgetting his wife's birthday by buying her five dozen roses.

etymology: Greek atrophia ('lack of nourishment'), from a- ('without') + trophe ('nourishment')
exampleSentence: If muscles do not receive enough blood, they will soon atrophy and die.

etymology: Old French ataindre, from Latin attingere ('to touch'), from ad- + tangere ('to touch')
exampleSentence: The athletes strived to attain their best times in competition.

etymology: Latin attribuere ('to assign'), from ad- + tribuere ('to grant, give')

senses.exampleSentence: He attributes all of his success to his mother's undying encouragement.
Among the beetle's most peculiar attributes is its thorny protruding eyes.

etymology: Greek a- ('not') + typos ('type, model'), via Latin typicalis
exampleSentence: Screaming and crying is atypical adult behavior.

etymology: Latin audax ('bold'), from audere ('to dare')
exampleSentence: The security guard was shocked by the fan's audacious attempt to offer him a bribe.

etymology: Latin audibilis, from audire ('to hear')
exampleSentence: The missing person's shouts were unfortunately not audible.

etymology: Latin augmentare, from augere ('to increase') — root of words like August and auction
exampleSentence: The eager student seeks to augment his knowledge of French vocabulary by reading French literature.

etymology: Latin auspicium ('omen from bird observation'), from avis ('bird') + specere ('to look') — Romans read omens from birds
exampleSentence: The tennis player considered the sunny forecast an auspicious sign that she would win her match.

etymology: Latin austerus, from Greek austeros ('harsh, severe'), from auein ('to dry')
exampleSentence: The austere furniture inside the abandoned house made the place feel haunted.

etymology: Latin avaritia, from avarus ('greedy'), from avere ('to crave')
exampleSentence: The banker's avarice led him to amass a tremendous personal fortune.

etymology: Old French avengier, from a- + vengier, from Latin vindicare ('to claim, avenge')
exampleSentence: The victims will take justice into their own hands and strive to avenge themselves against the men who robbed them.

etymology: Latin aversio, from avertere ('to turn away'), from ab- + vertere ('to turn')
exampleSentence: Because he's from Hawaii, Ben has an aversion to autumn, winter, and cold climates in general.

etymology: Old English balca ('ridge, beam'); figuratively a beam or obstacle that stops progress
exampleSentence: Edna's boss balked at her request for another raise.

etymology: Old French balade, from Provençal balada ('song for dancing'), from balar ('to dance'), from Latin ballare
exampleSentence: Greta's boyfriend played her a ballad on the guitar during their walk through the dark woods.

etymology: French banal ('compulsory, common'), from ban ('proclamation, obligatory service') — what all villagers shared was banal
exampleSentence: The client rejected our proposal because they found our presentation banal and unimpressive.

etymology: Old English bana ('killer, murderer, slayer'); evolved to mean that which causes ruin or misery
exampleSentence: Advanced physics is the bane of many students' academic lives.

definition: A poet, often a singer as well
etymology: Gaelic/Welsh bard — Celtic tradition of professional poets who memorized and recited epic tales
exampleSentence: Shakespeare is often considered the greatest bard in the history of the English language.

etymology: Middle English baischen ('to be abashed'), from Old French esbahir, from es- + bahir ('to astonish'); -ful suffix added in 16th century
exampleSentence: Frankie's mother told him not to be bashful when he refused to attend the birthday party.

etymology: Middle English, from be- + guile ('deception'), from Old Norse *vegla, related to Old English wil ('trick')
exampleSentence: The thief beguiled his partners into surrendering all of their money to him.

definition: Something of tremendous power or size
etymology: Hebrew behemoth — a massive creature described in the Book of Job, possibly a hippopotamus or mythical beast
exampleSentence: The new aircraft carrier is among several behemoths that the Air Force has added to its fleet.

definition: Marked by goodness or doing good
etymology: Latin benevolens, from bene ('well') + volens ('wishing'), present participle of velle ('to wish')
exampleSentence: Police officers should be commended for their benevolent service to the community.

etymology: Latin benignus ('kind, generous'), from bene ('well') + gignere ('to beget')
exampleSentence: We were all relieved to hear that the medical tests determined her tumor to be benign.

etymology: Old English becwethan, from be- + cwethan ('to say, declare') — formally declaring what one leaves behind
exampleSentence: Jon's father bequeathed his entire estate to his mother.

etymology: Middle English, from be- (intensive) + rate ('to scold'), of uncertain origin
exampleSentence: The angry boss berated his employees for failing to meet their deadline.

etymology: Past participle of bereave, from Old English bereafian ('to deprive'), from be- + reafian ('to rob')
exampleSentence: His family was bereft of food and shelter following the tornado.

etymology: Middle English bisechen, from be- + sechen ('to seek') — to urgently seek something from someone
exampleSentence: The servant beseeched the king for food to feed his starving family.

etymology: Old French biais ('slope, slant'), probably from Provençal; originally a bowling term for a ball rolling crooked
exampleSentence: The judge's hidden bias against smokers led him to make an unfair decision.

etymology: 17th century; possibly from balk or from a card game term meaning to thwart an opponent
exampleSentence: The lawyer discovered that this firm had bilked several clients out of thousands of dollars.

etymology: Old French blandiss-, from Latin blandiri ('to flatter, coax'), from blandus ('mild, flattering')
exampleSentence: Rachel's assistant tried to blandish her into accepting the deal.

etymology: Old French blemiss-, from blemir ('to make pale, injure'), of Germanic origin
exampleSentence: The dealer agreed to lower the price because of the many blemishes on the surface of the wooden furniture.

etymology: 17th century, of uncertain origin; possibly related to Old English blæce ('skin disease')

senses.exampleSentence: The potato blight destroyed the harvest and bankrupted many families.
His bad morale is a blight upon this entire operation.

etymology: Middle English boistuous ('crude, rough'), possibly from Old French boisteus ('lame, clumsy')
exampleSentence: The candidate won the vote after giving several boisterous speeches on television.

etymology: From bombast ('cotton padding'), from French bombace, from Medieval Latin bombax ('cotton') — speech that is stuffed with padding
exampleSentence: The singer's bombastic performance disgusted the crowd.

etymology: Old Norse bon ('prayer, request'); evolved to mean something granted as a favor
exampleSentence: The good weather has been a boon for many businesses located near the beach.

etymology: French bourgeois, from Old French burgeis ('town-dweller'), from bourg ('town'), from Latin burgus ('fort')
exampleSentence: Many businessmen receive criticism for their bourgeois approach to life.

etymology: Old English braesen ('of brass'), from bras ('brass') — brass was associated with hardness and boldness
exampleSentence: Critics condemned the novelist's brazen attempt to plagiarize Hemingway's story.

etymology: French brusque, from Italian brusco ('sour, rough'), possibly from Medieval Latin bruscus ('butcher's broom')
exampleSentence: The captain's brusque manner offended the passengers.

etymology: Old French buffeter, from buffet ('a blow'), of imitative origin

senses.exampleSentence: The strong winds buffeted the ships, threatening to capsize them.

senses.definition: An arrangement of food set out on a table
senses.exampleSentence: Rather than sitting around a table, the guests took food from our buffet and ate standing up.

etymology: Old French burniss-, from burnir ('to polish'), variant of brunir, from brun ('brown, shining')
exampleSentence: His mother asked him to burnish the silverware before setting the table.

etymology: Old French bouterez, from bouter ('to push, thrust'), from Frankish *botan — the arch that pushes against a wall

senses.exampleSentence: The column buttresses the roof above the statue.
The buttress supports the roof above the statues.

etymology: Greek kakophonia, from kakos ('bad') + phone ('sound, voice')
exampleSentence: The elementary school orchestra created a cacophony at the recital.

etymology: Italian cadenza, from cadere ('to fall'), from Latin cadere — the falling of pitch at the end of a musical phrase
exampleSentence: The pianist used the foot pedal to emphasize the cadence of the sonata.

etymology: French cajoler ('to cajole, chatter like a jay'), from Old French gaioler ('to chatter like a jay in a cage')
exampleSentence: Fred's buddies cajoled him into attending the bachelor party.

etymology: Latin calamitas ('damage, disaster'), possibly related to calamus ('stalk') — crop damage from storms
exampleSentence: The earthquake in San Francisco was a calamity worse than any other natural disaster in history.

etymology: From caliber, from Italian calibro, from Arabic qalib ('shoemaker's last, mold'); to measure the bore of a gun
exampleSentence: The mechanic calibrated the car's transmission to make the motor run most efficiently.

etymology: Latin callosus ('hard-skinned'), from callum ('hardened skin') — emotionally thick-skinned
exampleSentence: The murderer's callous lack of remorse shocked the jury.

definition: An attempt to spoil someone else's reputation by spreading lies
etymology: Latin calumnia ('false accusation, slander'), from calvi ('to trick, deceive')
exampleSentence: The local official's calumny ended up ruining his opponent's prospect of winning the election.

etymology: French camaraderie, from camarade ('comrade'), from Spanish camarada ('roommate'), from camara ('chamber'), from Latin camera
exampleSentence: Camaraderie among employees usually leads to success in business.

etymology: Latin candor ('whiteness, brilliance, frankness'), from candere ('to shine, be white')
exampleSentence: We were surprised by the candor of the mayor's speech because he is usually rather evasive.

etymology: Scottish/Northern English, from can ('to know'), from Old English cunnan ('to know, be able')
exampleSentence: The canny runner hung at the back of the pack through much of the race, then sprinted past them at the end.

etymology: Old French canevas, from Latin cannabis ('hemp'), from Greek kannabis — canvas was originally woven from hemp

senses.definition: A piece of cloth on which an artist paints
senses.exampleSentence: Picasso liked to work on canvas rather than on bare cement.
We canvassed the neighborhood looking for clues.

etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin capax, capac- 'capable' + -ious.
exampleSentence: The workers delighted in their new capacious office space.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from medieval Latin capitulat- 'drawn up under headings', from capitulum 'little head'.
exampleSentence: The army finally capitulated after fighting a long costly battle.

etymology: Early 17th century: from French capricieux, from Italian capriccioso.
exampleSentence: The young girl’s capricious tendencies made it difficult for her to focus on achieving her goals.

definition: To get the attention of, hold
etymology: Early 16th century: from late Latin captivat- 'taken captive'.
exampleSentence: The fireworks captivated the young boy, who had never seen such things before.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from German gar aus 'right out' (used of emptying a bumper).
exampleSentence: We caroused all night after getting married.

etymology: Middle English: from Old Norse karpa 'brag', later meaning 'find fault'.
exampleSentence: The husband divorced his wife after listening to her carping voice for decades.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French catalogue, from late Latin catalogus.

senses.definition: To list, enter into a list
senses.exampleSentence: The judge cataloged the victim’s injuries before calculating how much money he would award.
We received a catalog from J. Crew that displayed all of their new items.

etymology: Mid 19th century: back-formation from catalysis.
exampleSentence: The president’s speech catalyzed the nation and resuscitated the economy.

definition: A meeting usually held by people working toward the same goal
etymology: Mid 18th century (originally US): perhaps from Algonquian.
exampleSentence: The ironworkers held a caucus to determine how much of a pay increase they would request.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin causticus, from Greek kaustikos.
exampleSentence: The politicians exchanged caustic insults for over an hour during the debate.

etymology: Late 18th century (originally US): perhaps an alteration of curvet.
exampleSentence: The adults ate their dinners on the patio, while the children cavorted around the pool.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin censura 'judgment, assessment'.

senses.exampleSentence: The frustrated teenager could not put up with anymore of her critical mother’s censure.
The principal censured the head of the English Department for forcing students to learn esoteric vocabulary.

etymology: Early 19th century: from Latin cerebrum 'brain' + -al.
exampleSentence: The books we read in this class are too cerebral— they don’t engage my emotions at all.

etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin, from Greek khaos 'vast chasm, void'.
exampleSentence: Mr. Thornton’s sudden departure for the lavatory plunged his classroom into chaos.

etymology: Middle English: from obsolete chastie (from Old French chastier) + -ise.
exampleSentence: After being chastised by her peers for mimicking Britney Spears, Miranda dyed her hair black and affected a Gothic style.

definition: To feel or show affection toward something
etymology: Middle English: from Old French cheriss-, lengthened stem of cherir.
exampleSentence: She continued to cherish her red plaid trousers, even though they had gone out of style and no longer fit her.

etymology: Old English cīdan, of unknown origin.
exampleSentence: Lucy chided Russell for his vulgar habits and sloppy appearance.

etymology: Late 18th century: from Greek khoreia 'dancing in unison' + -graphy.
exampleSentence: The plot of the musical was banal, but the choreography was stunning.

etymology: Middle English: from Anglo-Norman French cronicle, from late Latin chronica.

senses.exampleSentence: The library featured the newly updated chronicle of World War II.
Albert’s diary chronicled the day-to-day growth of his obsession with Cynthia.

etymology: Late 16th century: from modern Latin chronologicus + -al.
exampleSentence: Lionel carefully arranged the snapshots of his former girlfriends in chronological order, and then set fire to them.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin circuitosus, from Latin circuitus.
exampleSentence: The bus’s circuitous route took us through numerous outlying suburbs.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin circumlocutio(n-), from circum 'around' + locutio.
exampleSentence: The professor’s habit of speaking in circumlocutions made it difficult to follow his lectures.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin circumscribere, from circum 'around' + scribere 'write'.
exampleSentence: The children were permitted to play tag only within a carefully circumscribed area of the lawn.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin circumspectus, from circumspicere 'look around'.
exampleSentence: Though I promised Rachel’s father I would bring her home promptly by midnight, it would have been more circumspect not to have specified a time.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin circumvent- 'baffled, enclosed', from circumvenire.
exampleSentence: The school’s dress code forbidding navel-baring jeans was circumvented by the determined students, who were careful to cover up with long coats when administrators were nearby.

definition: Able to perceive things that normal people cannot
etymology: Late 17th century: from French, from clair 'clear' + voyant 'seeing'.
exampleSentence: Zelda’s uncanny ability to detect my lies was nothing short of clairvoyant.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French clamour, from Latin clamor.

senses.exampleSentence: Each morning the birds outside my window make such a clamor that they wake me up.
Neville’s fans clamored for him to appear on stage, but he had passed out on the floor of his dressing room.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from French clandestin or Latin clandestinus.
exampleSentence: Announcing to her boyfriend that she was going to the gym, Sophie actually went to meet Joseph for a clandestine liaison.

etymology: Old English clēofan 'split'.

senses.exampleSentence: Following the scandalous disgrace of their leader, the entire political party cleaved into warring factions.
After resolving their marital problems, Junior and Rosa cleaved to one another all the more tightly.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin clementia, from clemens, clement- 'merciful'.
exampleSentence: After he forgot their anniversary, Martin could only beg Maria for clemency.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French clergie, based on ecclesiastical Latin clericus 'clergyman'.
exampleSentence: Though the villagers viewed the church rectory as quaint and charming, the clergy who lived there regarded it as a mildewy and dusty place that aggravated their allergies.

etymology: Late Middle English: from obsolete cloy 'to clog'.
exampleSentence: Though Ronald was physically attractive, Maud found his constant compliments and solicitous remarks cloying.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin coagulat- 'curdled', from the verb coagulare.
exampleSentence: The top layer of the pudding had coagulated into a thick skin.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin coalescere, from co- 'together' + alescere 'grow up'.
exampleSentence: Gordon’s ensemble of thrift-shop garments coalesced into a surprisingly handsome outfit.

definition: A person who makes or repairs shoes
etymology: Middle English: of unknown origin.
exampleSentence: I had my neighborhood cobbler replace my worn-out leather soles with new ones.

definition: To make somebody do something by force or threat
etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin coercere 'restrain'.
exampleSentence: The court decided that Vanilla Ice did not have to honor the contract because he had been coerced into signing it.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin cogent- 'compelling', from the verb cogere.
exampleSentence: Irene’s arguments in favor of abstinence were so cogent that I could not resist them.

etymology: Early 19th century: from obsolete cognizance 'knowledge' + -ant.
exampleSentence: Jake avoided speaking to women in bars because he was cognizant of the fact that drinking impairs his judgment.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin cohaerent- 'sticking together'.
exampleSentence: Renee could not figure out what Monroe had seen because he was too distraught to deliver a coherent statement.

etymology: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin collateralis, from col- 'together with' + lateralis.

senses.exampleSentence: Divorcing my wife had the collateral effect of making me poor, as she was the only one of us with a job or money.
Jacob left his watch as collateral for the $500 loan.

etymology: Mid 18th century: from Latin colloquium 'conversation' + -al.
exampleSentence: Adam’s essay on sexual response in primates was marked down because it contained too many colloquial expressions.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin collusio(n-), from the verb colludere.
exampleSentence: The three law students worked in collusion to steal the final exam.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin, from Greek kolossos.
exampleSentence: For 56 years, the ancient city of Rhodes featured a colossus standing astride its harbor.

definition: The act or process of burning
etymology: Late Middle English: from late Latin combustio(n-), from comburere 'burn up'.
exampleSentence: The unexpected combustion of the prosecution’s evidence forced the judge to dismiss the case against Ramirez.

definition: A notice of approval or recognition
etymology: Middle English: from Latin commendatio(n-), from the verb commendare.
exampleSentence: Jared received a commendation from Linda, his supervisor, for his stellar performance.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from late Latin commensuratus, from com- 'together' + mensuratus.
exampleSentence: Ahab selected a very long roll and proceeded to prepare a tuna salad sandwich commensurate with his enormous appetite.

etymology: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin commodiosus, from Latin commodus 'convenient'.
exampleSentence: Holden invited the three women to join him in the back seat of the taxicab, assuring them that the car was quite commodious.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin compellere, from com- 'together' + pellere 'drive'.
exampleSentence: Eliot’s speech was so compelling that Lenore accepted his proposal on the spot.

definition: To make an appropriate payment for something
etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin compensat- 'weighed against', from the verb compensare.
exampleSentence: Reginald bought Sharona a new dress to compensate her for the one he’d spilled his ice cream on.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin complacentia, from Latin complacere 'to please'.
exampleSentence: Colin tried to shock his friends out of their complacency by painting a frightening picture of what might happen to them.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin complementum, from complere 'fill up'.
exampleSentence: Ann’s scarf complements her blouse beautifully, making her seem fully dressed even though she isn’t wearing a coat.

definition: Ready to adapt oneself to another’s wishes
etymology: Mid 17th century: from comply + -ant.
exampleSentence: Sue had very strong opinions about what to do on a first date, and Ted was absolutely compliant.

definition: Being an accomplice in a wrongful act
etymology: Mid 20th century: back-formation from complicity.
exampleSentence: By keeping her daughter’s affair a secret, Maddie became complicit in it.

definition: An expression of esteem or approval
etymology: Mid 17th century: from French compliment, from Italian complimento.
exampleSentence: I blushed crimson when Emma gave me a compliment on my new haircut.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French compoune, from Latin componere 'put together'.

senses.exampleSentence: The difficulty of finding a fire escape amid the smoke was compounded with the dangers posed by the panicking crowds.
My attraction to Donna was a compound of curiosity about the unknown, physical desire, and intellectual admiration.

senses.definition: A walled area containing a group of buildings
senses.exampleSentence: When the fighting started, Joseph rushed into the family compound because it was safe and well defended.

etymology: Early 17th century: from French compréhensif, -ive, or late Latin comprehensivus.
exampleSentence: She sent me a comprehensive list of the ingredients needed to cook rabbit soufflé.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French compresser or Latin compressare, frequentative of comprimere.
exampleSentence: Lynn compressed her lips into a frown.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French componction, from ecclesiastical Latin compunctio(n-).
exampleSentence: He felt compunction for the shabby way he’d treated her.

etymology: Late 15th century: from French concéder or Latin concedere, from con- 'completely' + cedere 'yield'.
exampleSentence: Andrew had to concede that what his mother said about Diana made sense.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from late Latin conciliatorius, from conciliare 'combine, gain'.
exampleSentence: I took Amanda’s invitation to dinner as a very conciliatory gesture.

etymology: Late 16th century: from French concis or Latin concisus, past participle of concidere 'cut up, cut down'.
exampleSentence: Gordon did not like to waste time, and his instructions to Brenda were nothing if not concise.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin concoct- 'cooked together, digested', from concoquere.
exampleSentence: She concocted the most ridiculous story to explain her absence.

etymology: Early 17th century: from late Latin concomitant- 'accompanying', from concomitari.
exampleSentence: His dislike of hard work carried with it a concomitant lack of funds.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French concorde, from Latin concordia, from concors 'of one mind'.
exampleSentence: Julie and Harold began the evening with a disagreement, but ended it in a state of perfect concord.

definition: An expression of sympathy in sorrow
etymology: Early 17th century: from late Latin condolere 'suffer together' + -ence.
exampleSentence: Brian lamely offered his condolences on the loss of his sister’s roommate’s cat.

etymology: Mid 19th century: from Latin condonare 'refrain from exacting'.
exampleSentence: He refused to condone his brother’s crime.

definition: A pipe or channel through which something passes
etymology: Middle English: from Old French, from medieval Latin conductus 'pipe, channel'.
exampleSentence: The water flowed through the conduit into the container.

etymology: Middle English: from Latin confectio(n-), from conficere 'put together'.
exampleSentence: We went to the mall food court and purchased a delicious confection.

etymology: Mid 17th century: alteration of confident (as a noun), probably to represent the pronunciation of French confidente.
exampleSentence: Shortly after we met, she became my chief confidant.

etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin conflagratio(n-), from the verb conflagrare 'burn up'.
exampleSentence: The conflagration consumed the entire building.

etymology: Late Middle English: from late Latin confluentia, from confluere 'flow together'.
exampleSentence: A confluence of different factors made tonight the perfect night.

definition: One who behaves the same as others
etymology: Mid 17th century: from conform + -ist. Originally applied to someone conforming to the practices of the Church of England.
exampleSentence: Julian was such a conformist that he had to wait and see if his friends would do something before he would commit.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French confondre, from Latin confundere 'pour together, mix up'.
exampleSentence: MacGuyver confounded the policemen pursuing him by covering his tracks.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French congeler, from Latin congelare, from con- 'together' + gelare 'to freeze'.
exampleSentence: The sauce had congealed into a thick paste.

etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin con- 'together' + genialis 'festive'.
exampleSentence: His congenial manner made him popular wherever he went.

definition: A gathering of people, especially for religious services
etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin congregatio(n-), from congregare 'collect into a flock'.
exampleSentence: The priest told the congregation that he would be retiring.

definition: The quality of being in agreement
etymology: Late 15th century: from Old French congruite or late Latin congruitas, from congruus 'agreeing'.
exampleSentence: Bill and Veronica achieved a perfect congruity of opinion.

etymology: Early 17th century: from French conniver or Latin connivere 'shut the eyes (to)'.
exampleSentence: She connived to get me to give up my vacation plans.

definition: To dedicate something to a holy purpose
etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consecrat- 'dedicated, devoted as sacred', from the verb consecrare.
exampleSentence: Arvin consecrated his spare bedroom as a shrine to Christina.

etymology: Mid 19th century: from Latin, 'agreement', from consens- 'agreed', from the verb consentire.
exampleSentence: The jury was able to reach a consensus only after days of deliberation.

definition: To give something over to another’s care
etymology: Late Middle English: from French consigner or Latin consignare 'mark with a seal'.
exampleSentence: Unwillingly, he consigned his mother to a nursing home.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consolatio(n-), from the verb consolari 'to comfort'.
exampleSentence: Darren found Alexandra’s presence to be a consolation for his suffering.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French, or from Latin consonant- 'sounding together', from the verb consonare.
exampleSentence: The singers’ consonant voices were beautiful.

etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin constituent- 'establishing, appointing', from the verb constituere.
exampleSentence: The most important constituent of her perfume is something called ambergris.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French constraindre, from Latin constringere 'bind tightly together'.
exampleSentence: His belief in nonviolence constrained him from taking revenge on his attackers.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin construere 'build up, assemble', from con- 'together' + struere 'build'.
exampleSentence: He construed her throwing his clothes out the window as a signal that she wanted him to leave.

definition: To complete a deal; to complete a marriage ceremony through sexual intercourse
etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consummat- 'brought to completion', from the verb consummare.
exampleSentence: Erica and Donald consummated their agreement in the executive boardroom.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consumptio(n-), from the verb consumere 'use up'.
exampleSentence: Consumption of intoxicating beverages is not permitted on these premises.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin contemporaneus (from con- 'together with' + tempus, tempor- 'time') + -ous.
exampleSentence: Though her novels do not feature the themes of Romanticism, Jane Austen’s work was contemporaneous with that of Wordsworth and Byron.

definition: Having a tendency to quarrel or dispute
etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French contentieux, from Latin contentiosus, from content- 'striven'.
exampleSentence: George’s contentious personality made him unpopular with his classmates.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from late Latin contravenire, from Latin contra 'against' + venire 'come'.
exampleSentence: Edwidge contravened his landlady’s rule against overnight guests.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French contrit, from Latin contritus, past participle of conterere 'grind down'.
exampleSentence: Blake’s contrite behavior made it impossible to stay angry at him.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin contusio(n-), from the verb contundere 'to pound, bruise'.
exampleSentence: The contusions on his face suggested he’d been in a fight.

etymology: Late 16th century: a word of unknown origin, originally an Oxford University slang term for a pedant.
exampleSentence: Interpreting Jane’s behavior was a constant conundrum.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin convenire 'assemble, agree, fit', from con- 'together' + venire 'come'.
exampleSentence: Jason convened his entire extended family for a discussion.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin conventio(n-) 'meeting, covenant', from the verb convenire.

senses.exampleSentence: The hotel was full because of the cattleranchers’ convention.
The cattle-ranchers have a convention that you take off your boots before entering their houses.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin convivialis, from convivium 'a feast', from con- 'with' + vivere 'live'.
exampleSentence: The restaurant’s convivial atmosphere put me immediately at ease.

etymology: Late 18th century: past participle of convolute, from Latin convolutus 'rolled together'.
exampleSentence: Grace’s story was so convoluted that I couldn’t follow it.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French copieux or Latin copiosus, from copia 'plenty'.
exampleSentence: Copious amounts of Snapple were imbibed in the cafeteria.

etymology: Middle English: from medieval Latin cordialis, from Latin cor, cord- 'heart'.
exampleSentence: His cordial greeting melted my anger at once.

etymology: Late Middle English: from late Latin coronatio(n-), from coronare 'to crown'.
exampleSentence: The new king’s coronation occurred the day after his father’s death.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin corpulentia, from corpulentus 'fleshy', from corpus 'body'.
exampleSentence: Henry’s corpulence did not make him any less attractive to his charming, svelte wife.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin corroborat- 'strengthened', from the verb corroborare.
exampleSentence: Luke’s seemingly outrageous claim was corroborated by witnesses.

definition: Having the tendency to erode or eat away
etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French corosif, -ive, from late Latin corrosivus, from Latin corrodere 'gnaw through'.
exampleSentence: The effect of the chemical was highly corrosive.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Greek kosmopolitēs (from kosmos 'world' + politēs 'citizen') + -an.
exampleSentence: Lloyd’s education and upbringing were cosmopolitan, so he felt right at home among the powerful and learned.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from counter- 'against' + act.
exampleSentence: The antidote counteracted the effect of the poison.

etymology: Late 18th century: from French, from medieval Latin colpus 'blow'.

senses.exampleSentence: Alexander pulled off an amazing coup when he got a date with Cynthia by purposely getting hit by her car.

senses.definition: The overthrow of a government and assumption of authority
senses.exampleSentence: In their coup attempt, the army officers stormed the Parliament and took all the legislators hostage.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French cuveitier, based on Latin cupiditas 'cupidity'.
exampleSentence: I coveted Moses’s house, wife, and car.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French, past participle of covrir 'to cover'.
exampleSentence: Nerwin waged a covert campaign against his enemies, while outwardly appearing to remain friendly.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French credulite or Latin credulitas, from credulus 'believing'.
exampleSentence: His credulity made him an easy target for con men.

definition: A steady increase in intensity or volume
etymology: Late 18th century: Italian, present participle of crescere 'to grow', from Latin.
exampleSentence: The crescendo of the brass instruments gave the piece a patriotic feel.

definition: Standards by which something is judged
etymology: Early 17th century: from Greek kritērion 'means of judging', from kritēs 'judge'.
exampleSentence: Among Mrs. Fields’s criteria for good cookies are that they be moist and chewy.

definition: The climax toward which something progresses
etymology: Early 17th century: from late Latin culminatio(n-), from the verb culminare 'to crown'.
exampleSentence: The culmination of the couple’s argument was the decision to divorce.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French coupable, from Latin culpabilis, from culpa 'fault, blame'.
exampleSentence: He was culpable of the crime, and was sentenced to perform community service for 75 years.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin cultivat- 'prepared for crops', from the verb cultivare.
exampleSentence: At the library, she cultivated her interest in spy novels.

etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin cumulatus 'heaped up' + -ive.
exampleSentence: The cumulative effect of hours spent in the sun was a deep tan.

etymology: Middle English: perhaps from Old Norse kunnandi 'knowledge', from kunna 'know'.
exampleSentence: The general devised a cunning plan to surprise the enemy.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French cupidite or Latin cupiditas, from cupidus 'desirous', from cupere 'to desire'.
exampleSentence: His cupidity made him enter the abandoned gold mine despite the obvious dangers.

definition: Brief to the point of being superficial
etymology: Early 17th century: from French cursoire or late Latin cursorius 'of a runner', from Latin cursor 'runner'.
exampleSentence: Late for the meeting, she cast a cursory glance at the agenda.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin curtus 'cut short, abridged'.
exampleSentence: Her curt reply to my question made me realize that she was upset at me.

etymology: Late 15th century: from older curtal, from French courtault, from court 'short'.
exampleSentence: Since losing his job, he had to curtail his spending.

definition: Intimidating, causing one to lose courage
etymology: Middle English: from Old French danter, from Latin domare 'to tame'.
exampleSentence: He kept delaying the daunting act of asking for a promotion.

etymology: Middle English: from dear + -th. Original sense was 'shortness of food'.
exampleSentence: An eager reader, she was dismayed by the dearth of classic books at the library.

etymology: Early 19th century: from French débâcle, from débâcler 'unbar', from dé- 'un-' + bâcler 'to bar'.
exampleSentence: The elaborately designed fireworks show turned into a debacle when the fireworks started firing in random directions.

definition: To lower the quality or esteem of something
etymology: Mid 16th century: from de- 'down' + base, probably on the pattern of abase.
exampleSentence: The large raise that he gave himself debased his motives for running the charity.

definition: To corrupt by means of sensual pleasures
etymology: Late 16th century: from French débaucher 'turn away from one's duty'.
exampleSentence: An endless amount of good wine and cheese debauched the traveler.

definition: To expose the falseness of something
etymology: 1920s: from de- (expressing removal) + bunk.
exampleSentence: He debunked her claim to be the world’s greatest chess player by defeating her in 18 consecutive matches.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin decorus 'becoming' + -ous.
exampleSentence: The appreciative guest displayed decorous behavior toward his host.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from de- 'down' + cry, on the pattern of French décrier.
exampleSentence: The kind video rental clerk decried the policy of charging customers late fees.

definition: To ruin or injure something’s appearance
etymology: Middle English: from Old French desfacier, from des- (expressing removal) + face 'face'.
exampleSentence: The brothers used eggs and shaving cream to deface their neighbor’s mailbox.

etymology: Early 17th century: from medieval Latin diffamatorius, from diffamare 'publish a bad report'.
exampleSentence: The defamatory gossip spreading about the actor made the public less willing to see the actor’s new movie.

definition: To postpone something; to yield to another’s wisdom
etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French differer 'differ, defer', from Latin differre.
exampleSentence: Ron deferred to Diane, the expert on musical instruments, when he was asked about buying a piano.

etymology: Early 19th century: from deference + -ial.
exampleSentence: His deferential attitude toward her made her more confident in her ability to run the company.

etymology: Middle English: alteration of defoul, from Old French defouler 'trample down', influenced by file 'to foul'.
exampleSentence: She defiled the calm of the religious building by playing her banjo.

etymology: Middle English: variant of daft, in the sense 'meek'.
exampleSentence: Having worked in a bakery for many years, Marcus was a deft bread maker.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin defunctus 'dead', past participle of defungi 'carry out, finish'.
exampleSentence: They planned to turn the defunct schoolhouse into a community center.

definition: To hand over responsibility for something
etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin delegatus 'sent on a commission', from the verb delegare.
exampleSentence: The dean delegated the task of finding a new professor to a special hiring committee.

etymology: Mid 17th century: via medieval Latin from Greek dēlētērios 'noxious', from dēlētēr 'destroyer'.
exampleSentence: She experienced the deleterious effects of running a marathon without stretching her muscles enough beforehand.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin deliberatus, past participle of deliberare 'consider carefully'.
exampleSentence: Though Mary was quite upset, her actions to resolve the dispute were deliberate.

definition: To describe, outline, shed light on
etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin delineat- 'outlined', from the verb delineare, from de- 'out, completely' + linea 'line'.
exampleSentence: She neatly delineated her reasons for canceling the project’s funding.

definition: A leader who appeals to a people’s prejudices
etymology: Mid 17th century: from Greek dēmagōgos, from dēmos 'the people' + agōgos 'leading'.
exampleSentence: The demagogue strengthened his hold over his people by blaming immigrants for the lack of jobs.

definition: The marking of boundaries or categories
etymology: Early 18th century: from Spanish demarcación, from demarcar 'mark the bounds of'.
exampleSentence: Different cultures have different demarcations of good and evil.

definition: To lower the status or stature of something
etymology: Early 17th century: from de- 'completely' + mean (in the sense 'inferior').
exampleSentence: She refused to demean her secretary by making him order her lunch.

etymology: Late Middle English: perhaps from Old French demoure, past participle of demourer 'remain'.
exampleSentence: Though everyone else at the party was dancing and going crazy, she remained demure.

definition: To belittle, diminish the opinion of
etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin denigrat- 'blackened', from the verb denigrare, from de- 'completely' + niger 'black'.
exampleSentence: The company decided that its advertisements would no longer denigrate the company’s competitors.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French denoncier, from Latin denuntiare 'give official information'.
exampleSentence: The senator denounced her opponent as a greedy politician.

definition: To feel or express sorrow, disapproval
etymology: Mid 16th century: from French déplorer or Italian deplorare, from Latin deplorare.
exampleSentence: We all deplored the miserable working conditions in the factory.

etymology: Mid 17th century: alteration of obsolete pravity, from Latin pravitas, from pravus 'crooked, perverse'.
exampleSentence: Rumors of the ogre’s depravity made the children afraid to enter the forest.

etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin deprecat- 'prayed against', from the verb deprecari.
exampleSentence: Always over-modest, he deprecated his contribution to the local charity.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin derelictus 'forsaken', past participle of derelinquere.
exampleSentence: Even though it was dangerous, the children enjoyed going to the deserted lot and playing in the derelict house.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin deridere 'scoff at'.
exampleSentence: The bullies derided the foreign student’s accent.

definition: Taken directly from a source, unoriginal
etymology: Late Middle English: from French dérivatif, -ive, from Latin derivare 'draw off'.
exampleSentence: She was bored by his music because she felt that it was derivative and that she had heard it before.

definition: To violate the sacredness of a thing or place
etymology: Late 17th century: from de- (expressing reversal) + consecrate.
exampleSentence: They feared that the construction of a golf course would desecrate the preserved wilderness.

etymology: Late 16th century: from Latin desiccat- 'made thoroughly dry', from the verb desiccare.
exampleSentence: The skin of the desiccated mummy looked like old paper.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin desolatus 'abandoned', from the verb desolare.
exampleSentence: She found the desolate landscape quite a contrast to the hustle and bustle of the overcrowded city.

etymology: Late 17th century: from Latin despondent- 'giving up', from the verb despondere.
exampleSentence: Having failed the first math test, the despondent child saw no use in studying for the next and failed that one too.

definition: One who has total power and rules brutally
etymology: Mid 16th century: from French despote, from Greek despotēs 'master, absolute ruler'.
exampleSentence: The despot issued a death sentence for anyone who disobeyed his laws.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin destitutus 'forsaken', past participle of destituere.
exampleSentence: The hurricane destroyed many homes and left many families destitute.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin deterrere, from de- 'away from' + terrere 'frighten'.
exampleSentence: Bob’s description of scary snakes couldn’t deter Marcia from traveling in the rainforests.

etymology: From Latin devius ‘off the road,’ hence roundabout or dishonest.
exampleSentence: Not wanting to be punished, the devious girl blamed the broken vase on the cat.

etymology: Greek dialektos ‘discourse,’ via Latin dialectus.
exampleSentence: In the country’s remote, mountainous regions, the inhabitants spoke a dialect that the country’s other inhabitants had difficulty understanding.

etymology: Greek diaphanēs ‘showing through,’ from dia- + phainein ‘show.’
exampleSentence: Sunlight poured in through the diaphanous curtains, brightening the room.

etymology: Greek didaktikos ‘skilled in teaching,’ from didáskein ‘teach.’

senses.exampleSentence: She wrote up a didactic document showing new employees how to handle the company’s customers.
His didactic style of teaching made it seem like he wanted to persuade his students not to understand history fully, but to understand it from only one point of view.

etymology: Latin diffidere ‘to mistrust,’ hence lacking self-confidence.
exampleSentence: While eating dinner with the adults, the diffident youth did not speak for fear of seeming presumptuous.

etymology: Latin diffundere ‘pour out,’ from dis- + fundere ‘pour.’

senses.definition: To scatter, thin out, break up
senses.exampleSentence: He diffused the tension in the room by making a joke.
In her writings, she tried unsuccessfully to make others understand her diffuse thoughts.

etymology: Late Latin dilatorius ‘tending to delay,’ from differre ‘put off.’
exampleSentence: The general’s dilatory strategy enabled the enemy to regroup.

definition: Showing care in doing one’s work
etymology: Latin diligere ‘to love, esteem,’ extended to careful effort.
exampleSentence: The diligent researcher made sure to check her measurements multiple times.

etymology: Latin deminuere ‘lessen,’ via diminutivus ‘small-sized.’
exampleSentence: The bullies, tall and strong, picked on the diminutive child.

definition: A mournful song, especially for a funeral
etymology: Latin dirige ‘direct,’ opening word of a funeral chant.
exampleSentence: The bagpipers played a dirge as the casket was carried to the cemetery.

etymology: Latin dis- ‘apart’ + affectus ‘disposed,’ reversal of favorable feeling.
exampleSentence: Dismayed by Bobby’s poor behavior, the parents sent their disaffected son to a military academy to be disciplined.

definition: To deny knowledge of or responsibility for
etymology: Old French desavouer; Latin dis- + Medieval Latin avoware ‘declare.’
exampleSentence: Not wanting others to criticize her, she disavowed any involvement in the company’s hiring scandal.

etymology: Latin discernere ‘separate apart,’ hence distinguish.
exampleSentence: Though he hid his emotions, she discerned from his body language that he was angry.

etymology: Latin disclaudere ‘unclose,’ from dis- + claudere ‘close.’
exampleSentence: The CEO disclosed to the press that the company would have to fire several employees.

etymology: Middle French desconforter ‘rob of courage,’ confiture figuratively ‘comfort.’
exampleSentence: The normally cheery and playful children’s sudden misery discomfited the teacher.

definition: Not agreeing, not in harmony with
etymology: Latin discordant-, stem of discordāns, from discordia ‘disagreement.’
exampleSentence: The girls’ sobs were a discordant sound amid the general laughter that filled the restaurant.

definition: Difference, failure of things to correspond
etymology: Latin discrepantia ‘discord,’ from discrepare ‘sound differently.’
exampleSentence: He was troubled by the discrepancy between what he remembered paying for the appliance and what his receipt showed he paid for it.

definition: The quality of being reserved in speech or action; good judgment
etymology: Latin discretio ‘separation, discernment,’ from discernere.
exampleSentence: Not wanting her patient to get overly anxious, the doctor used discretion in deciding how much to tell the patient about his condition.

etymology: Latin discursus ‘running about,’ from dis- + currere ‘run.’
exampleSentence: The professor’s discursive lectures seemed to be about every subject except the one initially described.

etymology: Latin disdēns ‘feeling aversion,’ from dis- + dignārī ‘deem worthy.’

senses.definition: To scorn, hold in low esteem
senses.exampleSentence: Insecure about their jobs, the older employees disdained the recently hired ones, who were young and capable.
After learning of his immoral actions, Justine held Lawrence in disdain.

etymology: dis- + gruntled (obsolete ‘content’); playful formation modeled on earlier senses.
exampleSentence: The child believed that his parents had unjustly grounded him, and remained disgruntled for a week.

definition: Feeling a loss of spirit or morale
etymology: dis- + hearten; meaning loss of courage.
exampleSentence: The team was disheartened after losing in the finals of the tournament.

definition: To criticize or speak ill of
etymology: Old French desparagier ‘marry beneath rank,’ hence degrade.
exampleSentence: The saleswoman disparaged the competitor’s products to persuade her customers to buy what she was selling.

definition: Sharply differing, containing sharply contrasting elements
etymology: Latin disparātus ‘separated,’ from dis- + parāre ‘prepare as distinct.’
exampleSentence: Having widely varying interests, the students had disparate responses toward the novel.

definition: To send off to accomplish a duty
etymology: Italian dispacciare or Spanish despachar ‘send off hastily.’
exampleSentence: The carpenter dispatched his assistant to fetch wood.

etymology: Latin dispellere ‘drive apart,’ from dis- + pellere ‘drive.’
exampleSentence: She entered the office as usual on Monday, dispelling the rumor that she had been fired.

etymology: Latin dispersus ‘scattered,’ past participle of dispergere.
exampleSentence: When the rain began to pour, the crowd at the baseball game quickly dispersed.

definition: A state of being held in low regard
etymology: French désréputation; Latin repute replaced by disrepute pattern.
exampleSentence: The officer fell into disrepute after it was learned that he had disobeyed the orders he had given to his own soldiers.

etymology: Middle French dissembler ‘not resemble,’ mask true intentions.
exampleSentence: Not wanting to appear heartlessly greedy, she dissembled and hid her intention to sell her ailing father’s stamp collection.

etymology: Latin dissemināre ‘scatter seed,’ from dis- + semen ‘seed.’
exampleSentence: The politician disseminated his ideas across the town before the election.

etymology: Latin dissentīre ‘feel differently,’ from dis- + sentīre ‘feel.’

senses.exampleSentence: The principal argued that the child should repeat the fourth grade, but the unhappy parents dissented.
Unconvinced that the defendant was guilty, the last juror voiced his dissent with the rest of the jury.

etymology: Latin dissipāre ‘scatter,’ from dis- + supāre ‘to throw.’

senses.exampleSentence: The sun finally came out and dissipated the haze.
She dissipated her fortune on a series of bad investments.

etymology: Latin dissonantia ‘disagreement in sound,’ from dis- + sonāre ‘sound.’
exampleSentence: Though the president of the company often spoke of the company as reliant solely upon its workers, her decision to increase her own salary rather than reward her employees revealed a striking dissonance between her alleged beliefs and her actions.

definition: To persuade someone not to do something
