etymology: Latin dissuādēre ‘advise against,’ from dis- + suādēre ‘urge.’
exampleSentence: Worried that he would catch a cold, she tried to dissuade him from going out on winter nights.

etymology: Latin distendere ‘stretch apart,’ from dis- + tendere ‘stretch.’
exampleSentence: Years of drinking beer caused his stomach to distend.

etymology: Middle English didderen ‘tremble’; modern sense of indecision.
exampleSentence: Not wanting to offend either friend, he dithered about which of the two birthday parties he should attend.

etymology: Latin dīvīnus ‘of a god,’ from deus ‘god.’ Multiple senses converged in English.
exampleSentence: Terribly fond of desserts, she found the rich chocolate cake to be divine.

etymology: Latin dīvīs-, past stem of dīvidere ‘divide.’
exampleSentence: Her divisive tactics turned her two friends against each other.

etymology: Latin dīvulgāre ‘publish widely,’ from dis- + vulgus ‘common people.’
exampleSentence: Pressured by the press, the government finally divulged the previously unknown information.

etymology: Latin docilis ‘teachable,’ from docēre ‘teach.’
exampleSentence: She successfully taught the docile puppy several tricks.

definition: Aggressively and arrogantly certain about unproved principles
etymology: Greek dogmatikos ‘opinionated,’ from dogma ‘opinion, decree.’
exampleSentence: His dogmatic claim that men were better than women at fixing appliances angered everyone.

etymology: Latin dormīre ‘sleep,’ present participle dormiens.
exampleSentence: Though she pretended everything was fine, her anger lay dormant throughout the dinner party and exploded in screams of rage after everyone had left.

etymology: Latin dūrus ‘hard’ influenced Gaelic and Middle English senses.
exampleSentence: The children feared their dour neighbor because the old man would take their toys if he believed they were being too loud.

etymology: Latin dubius ‘wavering,’ from duo ‘two’ minds.
exampleSentence: Suspicious that he was only trying to get a raise, she found his praise dubious.

etymology: Latin duplicitās ‘doubleness,’ from duplex ‘twofold.’
exampleSentence: His duplicity involved convincing his employees to let him lower their salaries and increase their stock options, and then to steal the money he saved and run the company into the ground.

etymology: Latin dūrus ‘hard’ + neuter abstract formation via Old French duress.
exampleSentence: It was only under intense duress that he, who was normally against killing, fired his gun.

etymology: Greek dunamikos ‘powerful,’ from dunamis ‘power.’
exampleSentence: The parents found it hard to keep up with the dynamic music scene with which their children had become very familiar.

etymology: Latin ēbullīre ‘bubble out,’ from e- + bullīre ‘boil.’
exampleSentence: She became ebullient upon receiving an acceptance letter from her first-choice college.

definition: Consisting of a diverse variety of elements
etymology: Greek eklektikos ‘selective,’ from eklegein ‘pick out.’
exampleSentence: That bar attracts an eclectic crowd: lawyers, artists, circus clowns, and investment bankers.

etymology: Greek ekstasis ‘standing outside oneself,’ intense displacement.
exampleSentence: The couple was ecstatic when they learned that they had won the lottery.

etymology: Latin ēdictum ‘thing proclaimed,’ neuter past participle of ēdīcere.
exampleSentence: The ruler issued an edict requiring all of his subjects to bow down before him.

definition: To wipe out, obliterate, rub away
etymology: French effacer ‘literally smooth face away,’ from Latin ex- + facies.
exampleSentence: The husband was so angry at his wife for leaving him that he effaced all evidence of her presence; he threw out pictures of her and gave away all her belongings.

etymology: Latin effervēscere ‘begin to boil,’ from ex- + fervēre ‘boil.’
exampleSentence: My friend is so effervescent that she makes everyone smile.

etymology: Latin efficāx ‘effect-producing,’ from efficere ‘accomplish.’
exampleSentence: My doctor promised me that the cold medicine was efficacious, but I’m still sniffling.

etymology: French effronterie ‘shamelessness,’ based on effronté ‘barefaced.’
exampleSentence: When I told my aunt that she was boring, my mother scolded me for my effrontery.

etymology: Latin effulgēre ‘shine forth,’ from ex- + fulgēre ‘shine.’
exampleSentence: The golden palace was effulgent.

etymology: Latin ēgregius ‘standing out from the flock,’ from ex- + grex ‘herd.’
exampleSentence: The student who threw sloppy joes across the cafeteria was punished for his egregious behavior.

etymology: Latin ēlabōrāre ‘work out,’ later senses of ornament and complexity.
exampleSentence: Dan always beats me at chess because he develops such an elaborate game plan that I can never predict his next move.

etymology: Latin ēlātus ‘lifted up,’ past participle of efferre ‘carry out.’
exampleSentence: When she found out she had won the lottery, the writer was elated.

definition: A speech given in honor of a dead person
etymology: Greek elegeia ‘mourning verse,’ often sung with flute accompaniment.
exampleSentence: At the funeral, the widow gave a moving elegy describing her love for her husband.

definition: To bring forth, draw out, evoke
etymology: Latin ēlicere ‘draw out,’ from e- + lacere ‘entice.’
exampleSentence: Although I asked several times where the exit was, I elicited no response from the stone-faced policeman.

etymology: Latin ēloquēns ‘speaking out,’ from ēloquī ‘speak out.’
exampleSentence: The priest gave such an eloquent sermon that most churchgoers were crying.

etymology: Late Latin ēlūcidāre ‘make clear,’ from ē- + lūcidus ‘bright.’
exampleSentence: I didn’t understand why my friend was so angry with me, so I asked Janine to elucidate her feelings.

etymology: Latin ēlūdere ‘play away from,’ mock or slip past.
exampleSentence: Despite an intense search, the robber continues to elude the police.

etymology: Latin ēmaciāre ‘make lean,’ from ex- + maciēs ‘leanness.’
exampleSentence: My sister eats a lot of pastries and chocolate but still looks emaciated.

etymology: Old French embellir ‘make beautiful,’ from bel ‘beautiful.’

senses.exampleSentence: My mom embellished the living room by adding lace curtains.
When Harry told me that he had “done stuff” on his vacation, I asked him to embellish upon his account.

definition: To steal money by falsifying records
etymology: Anglo-French embeseiller ‘destroy, melt,’ shifted to secret theft.
exampleSentence: The accountant was fired for embezzling $10,000 of the company’s funds.

definition: To correct or revise a written text
etymology: Latin ēmendāre ‘free from faults,’ from ex- + mendum ‘fault.’
exampleSentence: If my sentence is incorrect, the editor will emend what I have written.

etymology: Latin ēminēre ‘stand out,’ from ex- + mineō ‘project.’

senses.exampleSentence: Mr. Phillips is such an eminent scholar that every professor on campus has come to hear him lecture.
There is an eminent stain on that shirt.

etymology: Latin ēmollīre ‘soften,’ from ex- + mollis ‘soft.’
exampleSentence: This emollient cream makes my skin very smooth.

etymology: Back-formation from emotion on pattern of verbs like vote.
exampleSentence: The director told the actor he had to emote, or else the audience would have no idea what his character was going through.

definition: Sensitivity to another’s feelings as if they were one’s own
etymology: Greek empatheia ‘passion,’ coined from em- + pathos ‘feeling.’
exampleSentence: I feel such empathy for my sister when she’s in pain that I cry too.

etymology: Greek empeirikos ‘experienced,’ from empeiria ‘experience.’

senses.exampleSentence: The scientist gathered empirical data on the growth rate of dandelions by studying the dandelions behind his house.

senses.definition: Capable of being proved or disproved by experiment
senses.exampleSentence: That all cats hate getting wet is an empirical statement: I can test it by bathing my cat, Trinket.

etymology: Latin aemulārī ‘strive to equal,’ from aemulus ‘rivaling.’
exampleSentence: I idolize Britney Spears so much that I emulate everything she does: I wear her outfits, sing along to her songs, and date a boy named Justin.

definition: To fill with love, fascinate, usually used in passive form followed by “of” or “with”
etymology: Old French enamourer ‘inspire with love,’ from en- + amour.
exampleSentence: I grew enamored of that boy when he quoted my favorite love poem.

definition: The audience’s demand for a repeat performance; also the artist’s performance in response to that demand
etymology: French encore ‘still, again,’ shouted for another round.
exampleSentence: At the end of the concert, all the fans yelled, “Encore! Encore!” but the band did not come out to play again.

etymology: Old French encombrer ‘block up,’ from Medieval Latin incombrāre.
exampleSentence: At the airport, my friend was encumbered by her luggage, so I offered to carry two of her bags.

etymology: Latin ēnervāre ‘cut sinews,’ metaphorically drain strength.
exampleSentence: Writing these sentences enervates me so much that I will have to take a nap after I finish.

etymology: Old French enfranchir ‘set free,’ from franc ‘free.’
exampleSentence: The Nineteenth Amendment enfranchised women.

etymology: Old French engendrer ‘beget,’ Latin ingenerāre ‘plant in.’
exampleSentence: During the Olympics, the victories of U.S. athletes engender a patriotic spirit among Americans.

etymology: Greek ainissesthai ‘speak in riddles,’ related to ainigma ‘riddle.’
exampleSentence: That man wearing the dark suit and dark glasses is so enigmatic that no one even knows his name.

etymology: Old French enemistié; Latin inimīcitia ‘unfriendliness.’
exampleSentence: Mark and Andy have clearly not forgiven each other, because the enmity between them is obvious to anyone in their presence.

etymology: French ennui ‘annoyance, boredom,’ from Latin in odio ‘in hatred.’
exampleSentence: I feel such ennui that I don’t look forward to anything, not even my birthday party.

definition: To include as a necessary step
etymology: Anglo-Norman entailer ‘cut in,’ restricting inheritance; extended to logical consequence.
exampleSentence: Building a new fence entails tearing down the old one.
The sailor’s stories of fighting off sharks and finding ancient treasures enthralled his young son.
etymology: Middle English *enthrallen*: *en-* ‘in’ + *thrall* ‘bondsman’; literally to hold in bondage, then to rivet attention.

exampleSentence: She promised she’d love me forever, but her “forever” was only ephemeral: she left me after one week.
etymology: Greek *ephemeros* ‘lasting a day,’ from *epi-* ‘upon’ + *hemera* ‘day.’

definition: Relating to or contained in letters
exampleSentence: Some people call me “Auntie’s boy,” because my aunt and I have such a close epistolary relationship that we write each other every day.
etymology: Latin *epistolaris*, from Greek *epistolē* ‘letter, message.’

exampleSentence: My mother, the epitome of good taste, always dresses more elegantly than I do.
etymology: Greek *epitomē* ‘a cutting into’ (abridgment), hence a compact summary; extended to a perfect representative.

exampleSentence: Even though he had just been fired, Mr. Simms showed great equanimity by neatly packing up his desk and wishing everyone in the office well.
etymology: Latin *aequanimitas*, from *aequus* ‘even’ + *animus* ‘mind.’

exampleSentence: His intentions were so equivocal that I didn’t know whether he was being chivalrous or sleazy.
etymology: Late Latin *aequivocus*, from Latin *aequus* ‘equal’ + *vox* ‘voice’—two voices at once.

exampleSentence: My Latin teacher is such an erudite scholar that he has translated some of the most difficult and abstruse ancient poetry.
etymology: Latin *eruditus* ‘well instructed,’ from *e-* ‘out’ + *rudis* ‘raw, untrained.’

exampleSentence: George hates the color green so much that he eschews all green food.
etymology: Middle English from Old French *eschiver*, akin to German *scheuen* ‘to shun, dread.’

definition: Understood by only a select few
exampleSentence: Even the most advanced students cannot understand the physicist’s esoteric theories.
etymology: Greek *esōterikos* ‘inner,’ comparative of *esō* ‘within’ (opposed to exoteric).

definition: To take up as a cause, support
exampleSentence: I love animals so much that I espouse animal rights.
etymology: Middle English from Old French *espouser* ‘to marry,’ from Latin *sponsus* ‘betrothed’; figuratively to take up a cause.

exampleSentence: In her flowing silk gown and lace veil, the bride looked ethereal.
etymology: Latin *aetherius*, from Greek *aithēr* ‘upper bright air.’

definition: The history of words, their origin and development
exampleSentence: From the study of etymology, I know that the word “quixotic” derives from Don Quixote and the word “gaudy” refers to the Spanish architect Gaudí.
etymology: Greek *etumologia*, from *etumon* ‘true sense’ + *logos* ‘account’—the study of a word’s true origin.

exampleSentence: I was euphoric when I found out that my sister had given birth to twins.
etymology: Greek *euphoria* ‘bearing well,’ from *eu-* ‘well’ + *pherein* ‘to bear.’

exampleSentence: My joy at getting promoted was evanescent because I discovered that I would have to work much longer hours in a less friendly office.
etymology: Latin *evanescere* ‘to vanish,’ from *e-* ‘out’ + *vanus* ‘empty.’

exampleSentence: Christopher’s hand-wringing and nail-biting evince how nervous he is about the upcoming English test.
etymology: Latin *evincere* ‘to conquer completely,’ then ‘to prove’; from *e-* ‘out’ + *vincere* ‘conquer.’

exampleSentence: The gruesome and scary movie I saw last night exacerbated my fears of the dark.
etymology: Latin *exacerbare*, from *ex-* ‘thoroughly’ + *acerbus* ‘harsh, bitter.’

exampleSentence: Michael Jordan is the figure in basketball we exalt the most.
etymology: Latin *exaltare* ‘to raise high,’ from *ex-* ‘up’ + *altus* ‘high.’

exampleSentence: George’s endless complaints exasperated his roomate.
etymology: Latin *exasperare* ‘to roughen,’ from *ex-* + *asper* ‘rough.’

definition: To dig out of the ground and remove
exampleSentence: The pharaoh’s treasures were excavated by archeologists in Egypt.
etymology: Latin *excavare*, from *ex-* ‘out’ + *cavus* ‘hollow.’

definition: To free from guilt or blame, exonerate
exampleSentence: My discovery of the ring behind the dresser exculpated me from the charge of having stolen it.
etymology: Latin *exculpare*, from *ex-* ‘from’ + *culpa* ‘blame.’

exampleSentence: After taking an excursion to the Bronx Zoo, I dreamed about pandas and monkeys.
etymology: Latin *excursio* ‘a running out,’ from *excurrere* ‘to run forth.’

exampleSentence: Her pudding is so execrable that it makes me sick.
etymology: Latin *execrabilis*, from *execrari* ‘to curse,’ *ex-* + *sacer* ‘sacred’ (invoke a curse).

exampleSentence: Henry exhorted his colleagues to join him in protesting against the university’s hiring policies.
etymology: Latin *exhortari* ‘to encourage strongly,’ from *ex-* + *hortari* ‘to urge.’

exampleSentence: The patient has an exigent need for medication, or else he will lose his sight.
etymology: Latin *exigens*, present participle of *exigere* ‘to demand,’ literally ‘drive out.’

definition: To free from guilt or blame, exculpate
exampleSentence: The true thief’s confession exonerated the man who had been held in custody for the crime.
etymology: Latin *exonerare* ‘to unload,’ from *ex-* + *onus* ‘burden’—remove the burden of guilt.

exampleSentence: Her exorbitant praise made me blush and squirm in my seat.
etymology: Late Latin *exorbitans* ‘going off track,’ from *ex-* + *orbita* ‘wheel track.’

exampleSentence: In his bid for reelection, the governor made an expedient move by tabling all controversial legislation.
etymology: Latin *expedire* ‘to free the feet,’ from *ex-* + *pes* ‘foot’—set free, dispatch, then advantageous.

exampleSentence: To expiate my selfishness, I gave all my profits to charity.
etymology: Latin *expiare*, from *ex-* ‘thoroughly’ + *piare* ‘to appease’ (*pius* ‘pious’).

exampleSentence: Fearful of an IRS investigation, Paul tried to expunge all incriminating evidence from his tax files.
etymology: Latin *expungere* ‘to prick out’ (cancel marks), from *ex-* + *pungere* ‘to prick.’

definition: To remove offensive or incorrect parts, usually of a book
exampleSentence: The history editors expurgated from the text all disparaging and inflammatory comments about the Republican Party.
etymology: Latin *expurgare* ‘to cleanse,’ from *ex-* + *purgare* ‘purify.’

exampleSentence: My mother’s extant love letters to my father are in the attic trunk.
etymology: Latin *extans*, present participle of *exstare* ‘to stand out, exist,’ from *ex-* + *stare* ‘stand.’

exampleSentence: Violet extolled the virtues of a vegetarian diet to her meatloving brother.
etymology: Latin *extollere* ‘to lift up,’ from *ex-* + *tollere* ‘raise.’

exampleSentence: Personal political ambitions should always remain extraneous to legislative policy, but, unfortunately, they rarely are.
etymology: Latin *extraneus* ‘external,’ from *extra* ‘outside.’

exampleSentence: Instead of trying to mediate between my brother and sister, I extricated myself from the family tension entirely and left the house for the day.
etymology: Latin *extricare*, from *ex-* + *tricae* ‘troubles, hindrances.’

exampleSentence: When she found out she won the literature prize, Mary exulted by dancing and singing through the school’s halls.
etymology: Latin *exultare* ‘to leap up,’ from *ex-* + *saltare* ‘to leap.’

definition: To make up, invent (When I arrived an hour late to class, I fabricated some excuse about my car breaking down on the way to school.) façade 1. (n.) the wall of a building (Meet me in front of the museum’s main façade.) 2. (n.) a deceptive appearance or attitude
exampleSentence: Despite my smiling façade, I am feeling melancholy.
etymology: Latin *fabricare* ‘to construct,’ from *faber* ‘smith, maker.’
Latin *facilis* ‘easy,’ from *facere* ‘to do’—done with ease, hence (disparagingly) too easy.

senses.exampleSentence: This game is so facile that even a four-yearold can master it.

senses.definition: Superficial, achieved with minimal thought or care, insincere
senses.exampleSentence: The business was in such shambles that any solution seemed facile at best; nothing could really helpit in the long-run.

exampleSentence: Emily offered me cigarettes on the fallacious assumption that I smoked.
etymology: Latin *fallaciosus*, from *fallere* ‘to deceive.’

definition: Meticulous, demanding, having high and often unattainable standards
exampleSentence: Mark is so fastidious that he is never able to finish a project because it always seems imperfect to him.
etymology: Latin *fastidiosus* ‘disdainful,’ from *fastidium* ‘loathing, squeamishness.’

exampleSentence: I cannot fathom why you like that crabby and mean-spirited neighbor of ours.
etymology: Old English *fæthm* ‘span of the outstretched arms,’ used of measuring depth.

exampleSentence: He considers himself a serious poet, but in truth, he only writes fatuous limericks.
etymology: Latin *fatuus* ‘foolish,’ akin to *faeri* ‘to speak’ as babble.

exampleSentence: The fecund tree bore enough apples to last us through the entire season.
etymology: Latin *fecundus* ‘fruitful,’ from *fe-/*fetus* ‘offspring.’
Latin *felix* ‘happy, fertile’ + *-ous*; suited to the occasion.

senses.exampleSentence: While his comments were idiotic and rambling, mine were felicitous and helpful.
I spent a felicitous afternoon visiting old friends.

exampleSentence: That beast looks so feral that I would fear being alone with it.
etymology: Latin *fera* ‘wild beast,’ from *ferus* ‘wild.’

exampleSentence: The fervent protestors chained themselves to the building and shouted all night long.
etymology: Latin *fervens* ‘boiling,’ present participle of *fervere* ‘to boil, glow.’

exampleSentence: I can tell from the fetid smell in your refrigerator that your milk has spoiled.
etymology: Latin *fetidus* ‘stinking,’ from *fetere* ‘to stink.’

exampleSentence: The dog was fettered to the parking meter.
etymology: Old English *feter* ‘chain,’ from Proto-Germanic *fetru*.

exampleSentence: In Greek dramas, the fickle gods help Achilles one day, and then harm him the next.
etymology: Middle English *fikel* ‘deceitful,’ from Old English *ficel* ‘pertaining to fraud.’

exampleSentence: Guard dogs are known for the great fidelity they show toward their masters.
etymology: Latin *fidelitas*, from *fides* ‘faith, trust.’

exampleSentence: Using figurative language, Jane likened the storm to an angry bull.
etymology: Middle English from Late Latin *figurativus*, from Latin *figura* ‘shape, figure.’

exampleSentence: Whenever I read an Agatha Christie mystery novel, I am always flabbergasted when I learn the identity of the murderer.
etymology: Colloquial 18th-c. British coinage; origin uncertain (perhaps playful blend).

exampleSentence: If a plant is not watered enough, its leaves become droopy and flaccid.
etymology: Latin *flaccidus*, from *flaccus* ‘flabby, hanging loose.’

exampleSentence: The judge’s decision to set the man free simply because that man was his brother was a flagrant abuse of power.
etymology: Latin *flagrans* ‘blazing,’ present participle of *flagrare* ‘to burn.’

exampleSentence: The writer’s florid prose belongs on a sentimental Hallmark card.
etymology: Latin *floridus* ‘flowery,’ from *flos* ‘flower.’

exampleSentence: I flouted the school’s dress code by wearing a tie-dyed tank top and a pair of cut-off jeans.
etymology: Perhaps from Middle Dutch *fluiten* ‘to whistle at’ in mockery; influenced by *flout* ‘jest.’

exampleSentence: Inspector Wilkens foiled the thieves by locking them in the bank along with their stolen money.
etymology: From obsolete *foil* ‘defeat’ (as in hunting), perhaps from *full* ‘to tread’; distinct from metal *foil.*

exampleSentence: When we got lost on our hiking trip, we foraged for berries and nuts in order to survive.
etymology: Old French *fourrage*, from *fuerre* ‘straw, fodder,’ Frankish *fodr* ‘food.’

exampleSentence: The doctor showed great forbearance in calming down the angry patient who shouted insults at him.
etymology: From *forbear* + *-ance*; Old English *forberan* ‘to endure.’

exampleSentence: I forestalled the cold I was getting by taking plenty of vitamin C pills and wearing a scarf.
etymology: Middle English *forestallen* ‘to intercept goods before market,’ from *fore-* + *stall* ‘marketplace.’

exampleSentence: Even though I had the flu, my family decided to go skiing for the weekend and leave me home alone, feeling feverish and forlorn.
etymology: Old English *forloren* ‘lost, past participle of *forleosan* ‘lose utterly.’

exampleSentence: My New Year’s resolution is to forsake smoking and drinking.
etymology: Old English *forsacan* ‘to renounce,’ from *for-* ‘completely’ + *sacan* ‘to dispute, deny.’

exampleSentence: Achilles’ fortitude in battle is legendary.
etymology: Latin *fortitudo*, from *fortis* ‘strong, brave.’

definition: Happening by chance, often lucky or fortunate
exampleSentence: After looking for Manuel and not finding him at home, Harriet had a fortuitous encounter with him at the post office.
etymology: Latin *fortuitus* ‘happening by chance,’ from *forte* ‘by chance,’ from * fors* ‘luck.’

definition: A medium for lecture or discussion
exampleSentence: Some radio talk-shows provide a good forum for political debate.
etymology: Latin *forum* ‘open square, public place,’ related to *foris* ‘outside.’

exampleSentence: To foster good health in the city, the mayor started a “Get out and exercise!” campaign.
etymology: Old English *fostrian* ‘to feed, nourish,’ from *fostor* ‘food, nourishment.’

exampleSentence: Although the child insisted he wasn’t tired, his fractious behavior—especially his decision to crush his cheese and crackers all over the floor—convinced everyone present that it was time to put him to bed.
etymology: From *fraction* in sense of discord; Latin *frangere* ‘to break’ + *-ous.’

definition: (usually used with “with”) filled or accompanied with
exampleSentence: Her glances in his direction were fraught with meaning, though precisely what meaning remained unclear.
etymology: Middle Dutch *vracht* ‘load’; English sense ‘laden (with danger)’ from maritime cargo.

exampleSentence: In the hours between night and morning, the frenetic pace of city life slows to a lull.
etymology: Old French *frenetique*, from Greek *phrenitis* ‘inflammation of the brain,’ from *phrēn* ‘mind.’

exampleSentence: Someday, all that anxiety about whether your zit will disappear before the prom will seem totally frivolous.
etymology: Latin *frivolus* ‘silly, empty,’ perhaps from *friare* ‘to crumble.’

exampleSentence: Richard is so frugal that his diet consists almost exclusively of catfish and chicken liver—the two most inexpensive foods in the store.
etymology: Latin *frugalis*, from *frux* ‘fruit, value’—worthy of the fruit of one’s labor.

exampleSentence: Jane’s placement of her drugs in her sock drawer was not as furtive as she thought, as the sock drawer is the first place most parents look.
etymology: French *furtif*, from Latin *furtivus*, from *fur* ‘thief.’

exampleSentence: Mrs. Watson has poor taste and covers every object in her house with a garish gold lamé.
etymology: Perhaps from obsolete *gair* ‘to stare’; influenced by *yellow* folk etymology; uncertain.

exampleSentence: Some talk show hosts are so garrulous that their guests can’t get a word in edgewise.
etymology: Latin *garrulus* ‘chattering,’ from *garrire* ‘to chatter.’

exampleSentence: Although he’s been known to behave like a real jerk, I would say that my brother is an overall genial guy.
etymology: Latin *genialis* ‘festive, pertaining to birth (*genius* spirit),’ from *genius* ‘attendant spirit.’

exampleSentence: Ada’s fried chicken tastes so divine, I don’t know how anyone can call gluttony a sin.
etymology: Old French *glotonie*, from Latin *glutto* ‘glutton.’

definition: To urge, spur, incite to action
exampleSentence: Jim may think he’s not going to fight Billy, but Billy will goad Jim on with insults until he throws a punch.
etymology: Old English *gād* ‘pointed stick,’ from Germanic *gaitō* ‘arrow, dart.’

definition: Someone fond of eating and drinking
exampleSentence: My parents, who used to eat little more than crackers and salad, have become real gourmands in their old age.
etymology: French *gourmand* ‘glutton,’ from Old French *gourmer* ‘to swallow greedily.’

exampleSentence: The student thought her grandiloquence would make her sound smart, but neither the class nor the teacher bought it.
etymology: Latin *grandiloquus* ‘grand-speaking,’ from *grandis* ‘great’ + *loqui* ‘speak.’

definition: On a magnificent or exaggerated scale
exampleSentence: Margaret planned a grandiose party, replete with elephants, trapeze artists, and clowns.
etymology: French *grandiose*, from Italian *grandioso*, from Latin *grandis* ‘great.’

exampleSentence: Every morning the guy at the donut shop gives me a gratuitous helping of ketchup packets.
etymology: Old French *gratuit*, from Latin *gratuitus* ‘free of charge,’ from *gratia* ‘favor, thanks.’

definition: Drawn to the company of others, sociable
exampleSentence: Well, if you’re not gregarious, I don’t know why you would want to go to a singles party!
etymology: Latin *gregarius* ‘belonging to a flock,’ from *grex* ‘herd, flock.’

definition: Injurious, hurtful; serious or grave in nature
exampleSentence: Electrocuting the inmate without being sure of his guilt would be a truly grievous mistake.
etymology: Anglo-French *grevous*, from *grever* ‘to burden,’ Latin *gravare* ‘make heavy.’

exampleSentence: Because of his great guile, the politician was able to survive scandal after scandal.
etymology: Middle English from Old French *guile*, of Germanic origin akin to *wile.*

exampleSentence: A girl can only hear “I love you” so many times before it begins to sound hackneyed and meaningless.
etymology: From *hackney* ‘ordinary riding horse for hire,’ extended to anything overused.

exampleSentence: In the hallowed corridors of the cathedral, the disturbed professor felt himself to be at peace.
etymology: Old English *halgian* ‘to make holy,’ from *hālig* ‘holy.’

exampleSentence: My poor, hapless family never seems to pick a sunny week to go on vacation.
etymology: From *hap* ‘luck’ (Old Norse *happ*) + *-less* ‘without.’
Middle French *harangue*, from medieval Latin *haringa* ‘troop in line’ (a military address).

senses.exampleSentence: Everyone had heard the teacher’s harangue about gum chewing in class before.
But this time the teacher harangued the class about the importance of brushing your teeth after chewing gum.

definition: Robust, capable of surviving through adverse conditions
exampleSentence: I too would have expected the plants to be dead by mid-November, but apparently they’re very hardy.
etymology: Middle English *hardi*, from Old French *hardi* ‘bold,’ from Frankish *hardjan* ‘make hard.’

exampleSentence: The car crash was a harrowing experience, but I have a feeling that the increase in my insurance premiums will be even more upsetting.
etymology: From *harrow* ‘to plow up’ (figuratively torment); Old English *hergian* ‘to harry.’

exampleSentence: The superstar’s haughty dismissal of her costars will backfire on her someday.
etymology: Extension of *haught* ‘high,’ from Old French *haut* ‘high,’ Latin *altus.*

definition: One who believes pleasure should be the primary pursuit of humans
exampleSentence: Because he’s such a hedonist, I knew Murray would appreciate the 11 cases of wine I bought him for his birthday.
etymology: Greek *hēdonē* ‘pleasure’ + *-ist’; coined for seekers of pleasure as a principle.

exampleSentence: Britain’s hegemony over its colonies was threatened once nationalist sentiment began to spread around the world.
etymology: Greek *hēgemonia* ‘leadership,’ from *hēgemōn* ‘leader,’ from *hēgeisthai* ‘to lead.’

exampleSentence: The killings were made all the more heinous by the fact that the murderer first tortured his victims for three days.
etymology: Old French *haineus*, from *haine* ‘hatred,’ from Frankish *hatis* ‘hatred.’

exampleSentence: I hate having only one flavor so I always buy the swirled, or should I say heterogeneous, type of ice cream.
etymology: Greek *heterogenēs*, from *heteros* ‘other’ + *genos* ‘kind.’

definition: A break or gap in duration or continuity
exampleSentence: The hiatus in service should last two or three months—until the cable lines are repaired .
etymology: Latin *hiatus* ‘opening, gap,’ from *hiare* ‘to gape.’

definition: A system with ranked groups, usually according to social, economic, or professional class
exampleSentence: Women found it very difficult to break into the upper ranks of the department’s hierarchy.
etymology: Greek *hierarchia* ‘rule of a high priest,’ from *hieros* ‘sacred’ + *archē* ‘rule.’

definition: Pretending to believe what one does not
exampleSentence: Once the politician began passing legislation that contradicted his campaign promises, his hypocrisy became apparent.
etymology: Greek *hypokrisis* ‘acting on a stage,’ from *hypo-* ‘under’ + *krinein* ‘to separate, judge.’

definition: Supposed or assumed true, but unproven
exampleSentence: Even though it has been celebrated by seven major newspapers, that the drug will be a success when tested in humans is still hypothetical.
etymology: Greek *hupothetikos*, from *hupotithenai* ‘to place under, suppose.’

definition: One who attacks common beliefs or institutions
exampleSentence: Jane goes to one protest after another, but she seems to be an iconoclast rather than an activist with a progressive agenda.
etymology: Greek *eikōn* ‘image’ + *klan* ‘to break’—one who smashes idols or conventions.

definition: Peculiar to one person; highly individualized
exampleSentence: I know you had trouble with the last test, but because your mistakes were highly idiosyncratic, I’m going to deny your request that the class be given a new test.
etymology: Greek *idios* ‘one’s own’ + *synkrasis* ‘mixture’—a personal blend of traits.

definition: Excessively worshipping one object or person
exampleSentence: Xena’s idolatrous fawning over the band—following them on tour, starting their fan club, filming their documentary—is really beginning to get on my nerves.
etymology: Greek *eidōlolatreia* ‘image worship,’ from *eidōlon* ‘image’ + *latreia* ‘service.’

exampleSentence: It was really ignominious to be kicked out of the dorm for having an illegal gas stove in my room.
etymology: Latin *ignominiosus*, from *in-* ‘not’ + *nomen* ‘name’—loss of good name.

exampleSentence: The fourth-grader learned many illicit words from a pamphlet that was being passed around school.
etymology: Latin *illicitus* ‘not allowed,’ from *in-* + *licitus* ‘permitted.’

exampleSentence: After breaking up with her boyfriend, Nancy decided to immerse herself in her work in order to avoid crying.
etymology: Latin *immergere*, from *in-* + *mergere* ‘to dip, plunge.’

exampleSentence: The laws of physics are immutable and constant.
etymology: Latin *immutabilis*, from *in-* + *mutare* ‘to change.’

exampleSentence: Stop being so impassive; it’s healthy to cry every now and then.
etymology: From *in-* ‘not’ + *passive* (Latin *pati* ‘to suffer, feel’).

exampleSentence: If your grades were as impeccable as your sister’s, then you too would receive a car for a graduation present.
etymology: Latin *impeccabilis*, from *in-* + *peccare* ‘to sin, stumble.’

exampleSentence: “I fear he’s too impecunious to take me out tonight,” the bratty girl whined.
etymology: From Latin *pecunia* ‘money’ + *in-* ‘without’—without money.
Late Latin *imperativus* ‘commanding,’ from *imperare* ‘to command.’

senses.exampleSentence: It is imperative that you have these folders organized by midday.
Her imperative to have the folders organized by midday was perceived as ridiculous by the others.

exampleSentence: The imperious nature of your manner led me to dislike you at once.
etymology: Latin *imperiosus* ‘commanding,’ from *imperium* ‘command, empire.’

exampleSentence: Most of your comments are so impertinent that I don’t wish to dignify them with an answer.
etymology: Latin *impertinens* ‘not belonging,’ from *in-* + *pertinere* ‘to pertain’—out of place.

exampleSentence: Because of their thick layer of fur, many seals are almost impervious to the cold.
etymology: Latin *impervius*, from *in-* + *pervius* ‘passable,’ from *per-* ‘through’ + *via* ‘way.’

exampleSentence: Hilda’s hasty slaying of the king was an impetuous, thoughtless action.
etymology: Late Latin *impetuosus*, from Latin *impetus* ‘attack, rush.’
Latin *impingere* ‘to drive in, strike against,’ from *in-* + *pangere* ‘to fasten, fix.’

senses.definition: To impact, affect, make an impression
senses.exampleSentence: The hail impinged the roof, leaving large dents.
I apologize for impinging upon you like this, but I really need to use your bathroom. Now.

definition: Incapable of being appeased or mitigated
exampleSentence: Watch out: once you shun Grandma’s cooking, she is totally implacable.
etymology: Latin *implacabilis*, from *in-* + *placare* ‘to appease.’
Late Latin *implēmentum* ‘filling up,’ from *implēre* ‘to fulfill’ (*in-* + *plēre* ‘fill’).

senses.exampleSentence: Do you have a knife or some other sort of implement that I could use to pry the lid off of this jar?

senses.definition: To put into effect, to institute
senses.exampleSentence: After the first town curfew failed to stop the graffiti problem, the mayor implemented a new policy to use security cameras to catch perpetrators in the act.

definition: To involve in an incriminating way, incriminate
exampleSentence: Even though Tom wasn’t present at the time of the shooting, he was implicated by the evidence suggesting that he had supplied the shooters with guns.
etymology: Latin *implicare* ‘to fold in,’ from *in-* + *plicare* ‘to fold.’

definition: Understood but not outwardly obvious, implied
exampleSentence: I know Professor Smith didn’t actually say not to write from personal experience, but I think such a message was implicit in her instruction to use scholarly sources.
etymology: Latin *implicitus* ‘folded in,’ past participle of *implicare*.

exampleSentence: Though the invaders used battering rams, catapults, and rain dances, the fortress proved impregnable and resisted all attacks.
etymology: Old French *impregnable*, from *in-* + *pregnable* ‘able to be taken’ (from *prendre* ‘take’).

exampleSentence: The impudent young man looked the princess up and down and told her she was hot even though she hadn’t asked him.
etymology: Latin *impudens* ‘shameless,’ from *in-* + *pudens* ‘modest,’ from *pudere* ‘to be ashamed.’

exampleSentence: The CEO imputed the many typos in the letter to his lazy secretary.
etymology: Latin *imputare* ‘to reckon, charge,’ from *in-* + *putare* ‘to think, reckon.’

exampleSentence: Some films are so inane that the psychology of the characters makes absolutely no sense.
etymology: Latin *inanis* ‘empty, void.’

definition: Incapable of expressing oneself clearly through speech
exampleSentence: Though he spoke for over an hour, the lecturer was completely inarticulate and the students had no idea what he was talking about.
etymology: Latin *inarticulatus* ‘not jointed,’ from *in-* + *articulare* ‘to speak distinctly.’
Latin *incarnare* ‘to make flesh,’ from *in-* + *caro* ‘flesh.’

senses.exampleSentence: In the church pageant, I play the role of greed incarnate.
The alien evaded detection by incarnating himself in a human form.

etymology: Latin *incendiarius*, from *incendium* ‘conflagration,’ from *incendere* ‘to set on fire.’

senses.exampleSentence: If we catch the incendiary who screamed “bomb” in the middle of the soccer match, we’re going to put him in jail.
Gas and lighter fluid are incendiary materials that should be kept out of hot storage areas.

exampleSentence: We wanted to go outside and play, but the incessantrain kept us indoors for two days.
etymology: Late Latin *incessans*, from *in-* + *cessare* ‘to cease.’

definition: Unformed or formless, in a beginning stage
exampleSentence: The country’s government is still inchoate and, because it has no great tradition, quite unstable.
etymology: Latin *inchoatus* ‘begun,’ from *inchoare* ‘to start work’ (origin disputed).

exampleSentence: The discussion wasn’t going anywhere until her incisive comment allowed everyone to see what the true issues were.
etymology: Latin *incisus* ‘cut into,’ from *incidere*, from *in-* + *caedere* ‘to cut.’

exampleSentence: Sarah has an inclination to see every foreign film she hears about, even when she’s sure that she won’t like it.
etymology: Latin *inclinatio*, from *inclinare* ‘to lean toward.’

exampleSentence: Only stubborn Tina would attempt to disprove the incontrovertible laws of physics.
etymology: Latin *incontrovertibilis*, from *in-* + *controvertere* ‘to dispute.’

exampleSentence: You can buy Grandma nicotine gum all you want, but I think that after sixty-five years of smoking she’s incorrigible.
etymology: Latin *incorrigibilis*, from *in-* + *corrigere* ‘to correct.’

definition: An enlargement; the process of increasing
exampleSentence: The workmen made the wall longer, increment by increment.
etymology: Latin *incrementum* ‘growth,’ from *increscere* ‘to grow upon.’
Latin *incumbere* ‘to lie upon,’ hence pressing as a duty.

senses.exampleSentence: The incumbent senator is already serving his fifth term.
It is incumbent upon this organization to offer aid to all who seek it.

exampleSentence: Even after traveling 62 miles, the indefatigable runner kept on moving.
etymology: Latin *indefatigabilis*, from *in-* + *defatigare* ‘to wear out.’

exampleSentence: Some fear that these plants, which are not indigenous to the region, may choke out the vegetation that is native to the area.
etymology: Late Latin *indigena* ‘native,’ from *indi-* ‘within’ + *gen-* ‘be born.’

exampleSentence: I would rather donate money to help the indigent population than to the park sculpture fund.
etymology: Old French *indigent*, from Latin *indigere* ‘to need,’ literally ‘to lack in.’

definition: Anger sparked by something unjust or unfair
exampleSentence: I resigned from the sorority because of my indignation at its hazing of new members.
etymology: Latin *indignatio*, from *indignari* ‘to regard as unworthy,’ from *in-* + *dignus* ‘worthy.’

exampleSentence: Why should my indolent children, who can’t even pick themselves up off the couch to pour their own juice, be rewarded with a trip to the mall?
etymology: Late Latin *indolens* ‘free from pain,’ from *in-* + *dolere* ‘to suffer’—avoiding effort.

exampleSentence: To be honest, Jim, my indomitable nature means I could never take orders from anyone, and especially not from a jerk like you.
etymology: Late Latin *indomitabilis*, from *in-* + *domitare* ‘to tame,’ frequentative of *domare*.

exampleSentence: Who knew that our decision to boycott school lunch would induce a huge riot?
etymology: Latin *inducere* ‘to lead in,’ from *in-* + *ducere* ‘to lead.’

definition: Unspeakable, incapable of being expressed through words
exampleSentence: It is said that the experience of playing with a dolphin is ineffable and can only be understood through direct encounter.
etymology: Latin *ineffabilis*, from *in-* + *effari* ‘to speak out.’

exampleSentence: She proved how inept she was when she forgot three orders and spilled a beer in a customer’s lap.
etymology: Latin *ineptus* ‘unsuited,’ from *in-* + *aptus* ‘fitted.’

definition: Incapable of being persuaded or placated
exampleSentence: Although I begged for hours, Mom was inexorable and refused to let me stay out all night after the prom.
etymology: Latin *inexorabilis*, from *in-* + *exorare* ‘to obtain by entreaty’—not moved by pleading.

exampleSentence: Unless I look at the solution manual, I have no way of solving this inextricable problem.
etymology: Latin *inextricabilis*, from *in-* + *extricare* ‘to disentangle.’

exampleSentence: The infamy of his crime will not lessen as the decades pass.
etymology: Latin *infamia*, from *in-* + *fama* ‘report, reputation.’

definition: An injection of one substance into another; the permeation of one substance by another
exampleSentence: The infusion of Eastern religion into Western philosophy created interesting new schools of thought.
etymology: Latin *infusio*, from *infundere* ‘to pour in,’ from *in-* + *fundere* ‘pour.’

exampleSentence: Her ingenious use of walnuts instead of the peanuts called for by the recipe was lauded by the other garden club members who found her cake delicious.
etymology: Latin *ingeniosus* ‘talented,’ from *ingenium* ‘innate quality, wit.’

exampleSentence: He must have writers, but his speeches seem so ingenuous it’s hard to believe he’s not speaking from his own heart.
etymology: Latin *ingenuus* ‘native, freeborn,’ hence frank and open.

exampleSentence: When I told you I needed the car last night, I certainly never meant to inhibit you from going out.
etymology: Latin *inhibere* ‘to hold in,’ from *in-* + *habere* ‘to hold.’

exampleSentence: I don’t see how I could ever work for a company that was so cold and inimical to me during my interviews.
etymology: Late Latin *inimicalis*, from Latin *inimicus* ‘enemy,’ from *in-* + *amicus* ‘friend.’

exampleSentence: “Your iniquity,” said the priest to the practical jokester, “will be forgiven.”
etymology: Old French *iniquité*, from Latin *iniquitas* ‘unfairness,’ from *iniquus* ‘unequal, unjust.’

exampleSentence: After his house was toilet-papered for the fifth time, the mayor issued an injunction against anyone younger than 21 buying toilet paper.
etymology: Late Latin *iniunctio*, from Latin *iniungere* ‘to enjoin,’ from *in-* + *iungere* ‘join.’

exampleSentence: His incredible athletic talent is innate, he never trains, lifts weights, or practices.
etymology: Latin *innatus* ‘inborn,’ past participle of *innasci* ‘to be born in.’

exampleSentence: In spite of their innocuous appearance, these mushrooms are actually quite poisonous.
etymology: Latin *innocuus* ‘harmless,’ from *in-* + *nocuus* ‘hurtful,’ from *nocēre* ‘to harm.’

definition: To do something in an unprecedented way
exampleSentence: Because of the stiff competition, the company knew it needed to pour a lot of energy into innovating new and better products.
etymology: Latin *innovare* ‘to renew,’ from *in-* + *novare* ‘to make new,’ from *novus* ‘new.’

exampleSentence: During the debate, the politician made several innuendos about the sexual activities of his opponent.
etymology: Latin *innuendo* ‘by nodding to,’ ablative gerund of *innuere* ‘to nod to, hint.’

definition: To introduce a microorganism, serum, or vaccine into an organism in order to increase immunity to illness; to vaccinate
exampleSentence: I’ve feared needles ever since I was inoculated against 37 diseases at age one; but I have also never been sick.
etymology: Latin *inoculare* ‘to graft an eye (bud),’ from *in-* + *oculus* ‘eye’ (bud of a plant).

definition: One who inquires, especially in a hostile manner
exampleSentence: The inquisitor was instructed to knock on every door in town in order to find the fugitive.
etymology: Latin *inquisitor*, from *inquirere* ‘to seek into,’ from *in-* + *quaerere* ‘ask.’

exampleSentence: My insatiable appetite for melons can be a real problem in the winter.
etymology: Latin *insatiabilis*, from *in-* + *satiare* ‘to fill.’

exampleSentence: Lisa’s insidious chocolate cake tastes so good but makes you feel so sick later on!
etymology: Latin *insidiosus* ‘cunning,’ from *insidiae* ‘ambush,’ from *insidere* ‘to sit in.’

exampleSentence: I wish Luke and Spencer would stop insinuating that my perfect report card is the result of anything other than my superior intelligence and good work habits.
etymology: Latin *insinuare* ‘to wind in,’ from *in-* + *sinus* ‘curve, fold.’

exampleSentence: The play was so insipid, I fell asleep halfway through.
etymology: French *insipide*, from Late Latin *insipidus*, from *in-* + *sapidus* ‘tasty,’ from *sapere* ‘to taste.’

exampleSentence: That celebrity is so insolent, making fun of his fans right to their faces.
etymology: Latin *insolens* ‘unusual, arrogant,’ from *in-* + *solere* ‘to be accustomed’—out of bounds.

exampleSentence: The demagogue instigated the crowd into a fury by telling them that they had been cheated by the federal government.
etymology: Latin *instigare* ‘to urge on,’ from *in-* + *stigare* ‘to prick, goad.’

definition: Separated and narrow-minded; tight-knit, closed off
exampleSentence: Because of the sensitive nature of their jobs, those who work for the CIA must remain insular and generally only spend time with each other.
etymology: Latin *insularis* ‘of an island,’ from *insula* ‘island.’

exampleSentence: The insurgent snuck into and defaced a different classroom each night until the administration agreed to meet his demands.
etymology: Latin *insurgere* ‘to rise up,’ from *in-* + *surgere* ‘to rise.’

exampleSentence: Without the integral ingredient of flour, you wouldn’t be able to make bread.
etymology: Late Latin *integralis*, from Latin *integer* ‘whole, untouched.’

exampleSentence: During our conversation, the cab driver occasionally interjected his opinion.
etymology: Latin *intericere* ‘to throw between,’ from *inter-* + *iacere* ‘to throw.’

definition: Someone who participates in a dialogue or conversation
exampleSentence: When the officials could not come to an agreement over the correct cover of the flags, the prime minister acted as an interlocutor.
etymology: Modern Latin *interlocutor*, from Latin *interloqui* ‘to speak between.’

exampleSentence: The fact that biology lectures came just before lunch made them seem interminable.
etymology: Late Latin *interminabilis*, from *in-* + *terminare* ‘to limit’—without limit.

exampleSentence: Mr. Brinford’s intimation that he would soon pass away occurred when he began to discuss how to distribute his belongings among his children.
etymology: Latin *intimatio* ‘an announcement, a hint,’ from *intimare* ‘to make known,’ from *intimus* ‘innermost, most familiar.’

exampleSentence: There was no end in sight to the intractable conflict between the warring countries.
etymology: Latin *intractabilis*, from *in-* + *tractare* ‘to handle, manage.’

definition: Refusing to compromise, often on an extreme opinion
exampleSentence: The intransigent child said he would have 12 scoops of ice cream, or he would bang his head against the wall until his mother fainted from fear.
etymology: Spanish *intransigente* (19th c.), from Latin *in-* + *transigere* ‘to come to an agreement’—refusing compromise.

definition: Brave in the face of danger
exampleSentence: After scaling a live volcano prior to its eruption, the explorer was praised for his intrepid attitude.
etymology: Latin *intrepidus* ‘fearless,’ from *in-* + *trepidus* ‘alarmed.’

exampleSentence: Because I am the star of a new sitcom, my fans are sure to inundate me with fan mail and praise.
etymology: Latin *inundare* ‘to overflow,’ from *in-* + *unda* ‘wave.’

definition: To cause someone or something to become accustomed to a situation
exampleSentence: Twenty years in the salt mines inured the man to the discomforts of dirt and grime.
etymology: Middle English *enuren*, from *in ure* ‘in use, in practice’—bring into use, accustom.

exampleSentence: My mother’s irrational invective against the way I dress only made me decide to dye my hair green.
etymology: Latin *invehere* (‘carry against’) thickened into *īnvectīva*, rhetoric’s abusive harangue.

exampleSentence: I’m the first to admit that I’m an inveterate coffee drinker—I drink four cups a day.
etymology: Latin *inveterāre* (‘grow old’) with *vetus* marks habits aged into seeming nature.

exampleSentence: Nobody was ever able to break into Batman’s inviolable Batcave.
etymology: Latin *violāre* means ‘hurt’; negatives shield rights or precincts barred from intrusion.

exampleSentence: At the smallest provocation, my irascible cat will begin scratching and clawing.
etymology: Latin *īra* (‘anger’) marks tempers keyed too hair-trigger-quick.

exampleSentence: The bride’s large diamond ring was iridescent in the afternoon sun.
etymology: From Greek *Îris*, the rainbow deity, hues glance across thin feathers or beetle wings alike.

exampleSentence: The irreverence displayed by the band that marched through the chapel disturbed many churchgoers.
etymology: *Reverēri* (‘revere deeply’) prefixed by *ir-* signals mockery pitched at sacred seriousness.

exampleSentence: The Bill of Rights is an irrevocable part of American law.
etymology: *Revocāre* (‘call back’) negated denies any revocation once the covenant sets.

exampleSentence: The crowd was jubilant when the firefighter carried the woman from the flaming building.
etymology: Latin *iūbilāre* carried victory shouts audible through festival noise.

exampleSentence: When the judicious king decided to compromise rather than send his army to its certain death, he was applauded.
etymology: Rooted in *ūdicium* (‘judgment’), the word praises temperate minds trimming risky zeal.

definition: The act of placing two things next to each other for implicit comparison
exampleSentence: The interior designer admired my juxtaposition of the yellow couch and green table.
etymology: Latin *iuxtā* means ‘near,’ setting contraries elbow-to-elbow so contrast leaps out.

definition: The solemn sound of a bell, often indicating a death
exampleSentence: Echoing throughout our village, the funeral knell made the stormy day even more grim.
etymology: Old English ringing tied ominous bells to ended lives—or ended eras—not merely news.

exampleSentence: After the performance, the reviewers gave the opera singer kudos for a job well done.
etymology: Greek *kŷdos* names renown others publicly grant a feat.

exampleSentence: Because he fell off his bike into a rosebush, the paperboy’s skin was covered with lacerations.
etymology: Latin *lacerāre* tears cloth or flesh; sarcasm can lacerate reputations with the same violence.

exampleSentence: The author’s laconic style has won him many followers who dislike wordiness.
etymology: *Lakonikos* recalls Spartan speech shorn to ruthless brevity.

exampleSentence: In the summer months, the great heat makes people languid and lazy.
etymology: *Languēre* drains vigor until motion feels honey-slow.

definition: Obtaining another’s property by theft or trickery
exampleSentence: When my car was not where I had left it, I realized that I was a victim of larceny.
etymology: Anglo-Norman *larcin* reaches back to *latrō* (‘robber’)—unlawful taking.

definition: The generous giving of lavish gifts
exampleSentence: My boss demonstrated great largess by giving me a new car.
etymology: French *large* (‘generous’) swelled gifts beyond obligatory minimum.

definition: Hidden, but capable of being exposed
exampleSentence: Sigmund’s dream represented his latent paranoid obsession with other people’s shoes.
etymology: *Latēre* cages potential until probing heat—or crisis—rips it outward.

exampleSentence: Such laudatory comments are unusual from someone who is usually so reserved in his opinions.
etymology: *Laudāre* heaps laureled praise voiced before audiences counting honor.
Images of flood-fed washing lent adjectives overstuffed with ornament or riches.

senses.exampleSentence: Because they had worked very hard, the performers appreciated the critic’s lavish praise.
Because the performers had worked hard, they deserved the praise that the critic lavished on them.

exampleSentence: Smuggling the French plants through customs by claiming that they were fake was a remarkable bit of legerdemain.
etymology: French literal ‘light of hand’ names nimble cheats on stage—or in dubious deals alike.

exampleSentence: Because Professor Oglethorpe allowed his students to choose their final grades, the other teachers believed that he was excessively lenient.
etymology: Latin *lēnis* matches gentle breezes judges borrow when sparing the lash.

definition: In a state of sluggishness or apathy
exampleSentence: When Jean Claude explained to his boss that he was lethargic and didn’t feel like working that day, the boss fired him.
etymology: Greek *lēthargía* suggests Lethe-heavy sleep drugging alertness away.
Anglo-Norman *lier* (‘bind’) makes people answer for money owed or duties breached.

senses.definition: Something for which one is legally responsible, usually involving a disadvantage or risk
senses.exampleSentence: The bungee-jumping tower was a great liability for the owners of the carnival.
Because she often lost her concentration and didn’t play defense, Marcy was a liability to the team.

definition: Advocating principles of liberty and free will
exampleSentence: The dissatisfied subjects overthrew the monarch and replaced him with a libertarian ruler who respected their democratic principles.
etymology: *Lībertās* steers politics that trim state coercion beside personal sovereignty.

definition: Displaying a lack of moral or legal restraints
exampleSentence: Marilee has always been fascinated by the licentious private lives of politicians.
etymology: *Licentia* slid from permission to shameless license without moral brakes.

exampleSentence: Mr. Johnson’s limpid writing style greatly pleased readers who disliked complicated novels.
etymology: *Limpidus* praises glass-clear streams or prose free of murk.

definition: Something that holds separate parts together
exampleSentence: The linchpin in the prosecution’s case was the hair from the defendant’s head, which was found at the scene of the crime.
etymology: Old smithing slang named the pin locking wheels; entire systems still pivot on one brace.

exampleSentence: Although the dancers were all outstanding, Jae Sun’s control of her lithe body was particularly impressive.
etymology: Germanic softness bends dancers through narrow choreography without strain.

exampleSentence: When the litigants began screaming at each other, Judge Koch ordered them to be silent.
etymology: *Lītigāre* means contest in court; dockets caption whichever actor wages the suit.

exampleSentence: Because Guenevere’s essay was so lucid, I only had to read it once to understand her reasoning.
etymology: *Lūcidus* comes from *lūx*—thought or prose that throws clarifying light through fog.

exampleSentence: The light of the luminous moon graced the shoulders of the beautiful maiden.
etymology: Light’s Latin root names matter—or metaphors—that visibly throw brilliance outward.

exampleSentence: Gideon’s story, in which he described a character torturing his sister’s dolls, was judged too lurid to be printed in the school’s literary magazine.
etymology: *Lūridus* began as sickly-yellow dread; tabloids borrow that corpse-light glare.

definition: A destructive whirlpool which rapidly sucks in objects
exampleSentence: Little did the explorers know that as they turned the next bend of the calm river a vicious maelstrom would catch their boat.
etymology: Dutch *maalstrom* fused grinding tides with corridors that swallowed ships.

exampleSentence: Although I had already broken most of her dishes, Jacqueline was magnanimous enough to continue letting me use them.
etymology: *Magnus animus* widens spirits past petty scorekeeping toward costly mercy.

exampleSentence: When I was arrested for speeding, I screamed maledictions against the policeman and the entire police department.
etymology: *Male dicere* is ‘speak badly,’ conjuring harm aloud rather than muted silence.

exampleSentence: The malevolent old man sat in the park all day, tripping unsuspecting passersby with his cane.
etymology: *Male velle* names wishing ill—hostility aimed with intent.

definition: Capable of being shaped or transformed
exampleSentence: Maximillian’s political opinions were so malleable that anyone he talked to was able to change his mind instantly.
etymology: *Malleus* hammers metal—or minds—into fresh shapes under pressure.

exampleSentence: In the Old Testament, God mandates that no one should steal.
etymology: *Mandāre* entrusts orders binding officials to lawful courses.
*Manifestus* suggests truth struck into open sight like cargo hauled on deck.

senses.exampleSentence: When I wrote the wrong sum on the chalkboard, my mistake was so manifest that the entire class burst into laughter.
His illness first manifested itself with particularly violent hiccups.

exampleSentence: The popularity of Dante’s Inferno is partly due to the fact that the work allows for manifold interpretations.
etymology: Old English *mani* plus folding tallied stacked layers of variety.

exampleSentence: Although many people enjoy romantic comedies, I usually find them maudlin and shallow.
etymology: Slurred devotion to weeping Magdalene images slid into cheap public sniffles.

exampleSentence: Andreas is a real maverick and always does things his own way.
etymology: Unbranded calves named Maverick became shorthand for stubborn autonomy.

exampleSentence: Although some nineteenthcentury critics viewed Dickens’s writing as mawkish, contemporary readers have found great emotional depth in his works.
etymology: Hints of maggot-soft decay color sentiment curdled by excess syrup.

definition: A common saying expressing a principle of conduct
exampleSentence: Miss Manners’s etiquette maxims are both entertaining and instructional.
etymology: *Maxima* capped portable rules distilled from lived wisdom.

exampleSentence: My meager portion of food did nothing to satisfy my appetite.
etymology: *Macer* pictured lean hunger—resources starved to bare threads.

exampleSentence: Susannah’s wardrobe contained an astonishing medley of colors, from olive green to fluorescent pink.
etymology: Musicians braided incongruent tunes into one swirling performance still called medley.

exampleSentence: The mendaciouscontent of the tabloid magazines is at least entertaining.
etymology: *Mendāx* ties fault-born liars bending truth with intent.

definition: Characterized by rapid change or temperamentality
exampleSentence: Though he was widely respected for his mathematical proofs, the mercurial genius was impossible to live with.
etymology: Roman *Mercūrius* lent fleet-foot mood swings that flip before witnesses settle.

exampleSentence: Manfred was given the congressional medal of honor for his meritorious actions.
etymology: *Meritus* celebrates fairly earned honor rather than rumor alone.

definition: The change of form, shape, substance
exampleSentence: Winnifred went to the gym every day for a year and underwent a metamorphosis from a waiflike girl to an athletic woman.
etymology: Greek *metá-* with *morphḗ* catalogs cocoon-to-moth refashionings utter and complete.

exampleSentence: The ornate needlework in the bride’s gown was a product of meticulous handiwork.
etymology: Latin care roots refused half measures—proofreaders live here.

exampleSentence: When I had an awful sore throat, only warm tea would mitigate the pain.
etymology: *Mitis* (‘mild’) calms harsh edges of blame, pain, or sentence alike.
*Moderārī* sizes passions until extremes stop scorching judgment.

senses.exampleSentence: Luckily, the restaurant we chose had moderate prices; none of us have any money.
Because he found both the liberal and conservative proposals too excessive, Mr. Park sided with the moderates.

exampleSentence: Refusing to display even a modicum of sensitivity, Henrietta announced her boss’s affair in front of the entire office.
etymology: *Modicus* measures stingy-but-sufficient pinches of what is needed.

definition: To pass from one state to another, especially in music
exampleSentence: The composer wrote a piece that modulated between minor and major keys.
etymology: *Modulus* turns dials on pitch, volume, tone until balance returns.

exampleSentence: The police officer mollified the angry woman by giving her a warning instead of a ticket.
etymology: *Mollis* supplies softness sponged atop bristling tempers until edges dull.

definition: A wet swampy bog; figuratively, something that traps and confuses
exampleSentence: When Theresa lost her job, she could not get out of her financial morass.
etymology: Dutch peat bogs suggested sucking ground where plans or morale sink without traction.

definition: The moral attitudes and fixed customs of a group of people.
exampleSentence: Mores change over time; many things that were tolerated in 1975 are no longer seen as being socially acceptable.
etymology: Latin *mōrēs* gathers habitual practices that carry society’s unwritten weight.

exampleSentence: Jason’s morose nature made him very unpleasant to talk to.
etymology: Latin *mōrōsus* trapped cheer inside chronic, brooding gloom.

exampleSentence: This Swiss Army knife has multifarious functions and capabilities. Among other things, it can act as a knife, a saw, a toothpick, and a slingshot.
etymology: Latin *multus* stacked with *-fari* kinds inventories dizzying variety.

definition: Concerned with the world rather than with heaven, commonplace
exampleSentence: He is more concerned with the mundane issues of day-to-day life than with spiritual topics.
etymology: *Mundus* slides from worldly to drably everyday when wonder is stripped away.

exampleSentence: The royal family’s munificence made everyone else in their country rich.
etymology: Latin *mūnus* plus *facere* builds giving that opens wide without counting change.

exampleSentence: Because fashion is so mutable, what is trendy today will look outdated in five years.
etymology: *Mutāre* bends states the way wax yields to warm fingers.

definition: Consisting of a very great number
exampleSentence: It was difficult to decide what to do Friday night because the city presented us with myriad possibilities for fun.
etymology: Greek *muriós* once tallied hosts too huge to number by hand.

exampleSentence: My day was boring, but the nadir came when I accidentally spilled a bowl of spaghetti on my head.
etymology: Arabic astronomy’s *nadīr* lies opposite the zenith—metaphor sinks to worst hour.

definition: In the process of being born or coming into existence
exampleSentence: Unfortunately, my brilliant paper was only in its nascent form on the morning that it was due.
etymology: Latin *nāscī* (‘be born’) tags ideas still wet with first breath.

exampleSentence: The transition between governments meant that who was actually in charge was a nebulous matter.
etymology: *Nebula* smoke leaves outlines soft as fog across telescopes.

exampleSentence: Although Dr. Meanman’s nefarious plot to melt the polar icecaps was terrifying, it was so impractical that nobody really worried about it.
etymology: Latin *nefās* names acts against divine or moral law.

exampleSentence: Jessie’s grandfather called me a negligent fool after I left the door to his apartment unlocked even though there had been a recent string of robberies.
etymology: *Neg-* plus *legere* suggests duties never picked up or chosen.

definition: Someone who is young or inexperienced
exampleSentence: As a neophyte in the literary world, Malik had trouble finding a publisher for his first novel.
etymology: Greek *neos* plus *phutón* pictures a newly planted sprout in doctrine’s garden.

definition: Relating to or occurring during the night
exampleSentence: Jackie was a nocturnal person; she would study until dawn and sleep until the evening.
etymology: *Nox* schedules creatures active while the sun sleeps.

definition: Unpleasant, offensive, especially to the sense of smell
exampleSentence: Nobody would enter the stalls until the horse’s noisome leavings were taken away.
etymology: Middle English revulsion roots tie stench to moral offense—not mere loudness.

exampleSentence: In the first six months after college, Jose led a nomadic life, living in New York, California, and Idaho.
etymology: Greek *nomeús* herded flocks between pastures without fixed hearths.

exampleSentence: Because he was moving the following week and needed to get rid of his furniture more than he needed money, Jordan sold everything for a nominal fee.
etymology: Latin *nomen* attaches labels divorced from substantive power underneath.

definition: Having a lack of concern, indifference
exampleSentence: Although deep down she was very angry, Marsha acted in a nonchalant manner when she found out that her best friend had used her clothing without asking.
etymology: French *nonchaloir* shrugged refusal to worry—cool boredom worn on purpose.

exampleSentence: I was surprised when I saw the movie star in person because she looked nondescript.
etymology: Anything too bland to merit description hides in beige crowds.

exampleSentence: Jacob was notorious for always arriving late at parties.
etymology: *Notus* widened from merely known toward widely infamous repute.

definition: A beginner, someone without training or experience
exampleSentence: Because we were all novices at yoga, our instructor decided to begin with the basics.
etymology: Latin *novus* stamps hands still clumsy with fresh craft.

exampleSentence: Environmentalists showed that the noxious weeds were destroying the insects’ natural habitats.
etymology: *Nocēre* catalogs harms borne by lungs, reputations, or soil alike.

definition: A slight variation in meaning, tone, expression
exampleSentence: The nuances of the poem were not obvious to the casual reader, but the professor was able to point them out.
etymology: French *nuer* coaxed distinctions finer than careless eyes register.

exampleSentence: Although Serena had never watered the plant, which was about to die, Javier was able to nurture it back to life.
etymology: Latin *nūtrīre* fed bodies—or characters—until habit took root.

definition: Unyielding to persuasion or moral influences
exampleSentence: The obdurate old man refused to take pity on the kittens.
etymology: *Obdurātus*, hammer-hardened, withstands coaxing pity or argument alike.

exampleSentence: The detective did want to answer the newspaperman’s questions, so he obfuscated the truth.
etymology: *Ob-* drapes shadow over prose meant to dizzy auditors.

definition: Diverging from a straight line or course, not straightforward
exampleSentence: Martin’s oblique language confused those who listened to him.
etymology: Latin *obliquus* slides at angles rather than confronting head-on.

definition: Lacking consciousness or awareness of something
exampleSentence: Oblivious to the burning smell emanating from the kitchen, my father did not notice that the rolls in the oven were burned until much too late.
etymology: *Oblīvīscī* swallows surroundings until nothing peripheral registers.

exampleSentence: Because he was standing in the shadows, his features were obscure.
etymology: *Obscurus* piles shadow until interpreters must grope deliberately.

exampleSentence: Mark acted like Janet’s servant, obeying her every request in an obsequious manner.
etymology: *Ob-* plus *sequī* describes followers tripping over themselves to please power.

definition: No longer used, out of date
exampleSentence: With the inventions of tape decks and CDs, which both have better sound and are easier to use, eight-track players are now entirely obsolete.
etymology: *Obsolesce* marks tools or words shelved dusty past usefulness.

exampleSentence: The obstinate child refused to leave the store until his mother bought him a candy bar.
etymology: *Obstināre* plants feet like stakes against persuasion’s wind.

exampleSentence: Billy’s obstreperous behavior prompted the librarian to ask him to leave the reading room.
etymology: Latin noise roots named crowds that shout down order itself.

definition: Lacking quickness of sensibility or intellect
exampleSentence: Political opponents warned that the prime minister’s obtuse approach to foreign policy would embroil the nation in mindless war.
etymology: *Obtūsus* blunted edges—whether angles or minds missing nuance.

exampleSentence: Mark was assigned the odious task of cleaning the cat’s litter box.
etymology: *Odisse* names hatred plain on the tongue.

definition: Offering one’s services when they are neither wanted nor needed
exampleSentence: Brenda resented Allan’s officious behavior when he selected colors that might best improve her artwork.
etymology: Latin *officium* twisted into busybody zeal ‘helping’ without welcome.

exampleSentence: The fortuneteller’s ominous words flashed through my mind as the hooded figure approached me in the alley.
etymology: *Omen* shadows events that feel heavy with coming harm.

exampleSentence: My parents lamented that the pleasures of living in a beautiful country estate no longer outweighed the onerous mortgage payments.
etymology: Latin *onus* loads backs until every step costs effort.

definition: Characterized by rich abundance verging on ostentation
exampleSentence: The opulent furnishings of the dictator’s private compound contrasted harshly with the meager accommodations of her subjects.
etymology: Latin *ops* (‘wealth’) spills outward in visible excess.

definition: A speech delivered in a formal or ceremonious manner
exampleSentence: The prime minister was visibly shaken when the unruly parliament interrupted his oration about failed domestic policies.
etymology: Latin *ōrāre* supplied formal speech measured for public ears.

exampleSentence: The ornate styling of the new model of luxury car could not compensate for the poor quality of its motor.
etymology: Ornament stacked on ornament until surfaces glitter with filigree.

exampleSentence: The company’s profits dwindled because the management pursued orthodox business policies that were incompatible with new industrial trends.
etymology: Greek *orthos* plus *doxa* aligned belief with received teaching.

definition: To sway from one side to the other
exampleSentence: My uncle oscillated between buying a station wagon to transport his family and buying a sports car to satisfy his boyhood fantasies.
etymology: Latin *oscillum* was a little swinging mask—motion that never locks still.

exampleSentence: Jack’s ostensible reason for driving was that airfare was too expensive, but in reality, he was afraid of flying.
etymology: What shows on the surface may cloak motives still tucked below deck.

exampleSentence: On the palace tour, the guide focused on the ostentatious decorations and spoke little of the royal family’s history.
etymology: Display curdles into performance meant to dazzle wallets or rivals.

exampleSentence: Beth risked ostracism if her roommates discovered her flatulence.
etymology: Athenian potsherd votes could banish citizens—metaphor still expels pariahs.

exampleSentence: The chemistry professor’s pacific demeanor helped the class remain calm after the experiment exploded.
etymology: Latin *pāx* steers temper toward dove-gray calm instead of war heat.

definition: Agreeable to the taste or sensibilities
exampleSentence: Despite the unpleasant smell, the exotic cheese was quite palatable.
etymology: *Palātum* tests whether ideas or flavors please the tongue—or mind.

definition: A range of colors or qualities
exampleSentence: The palette of colors utilized in the painting was equaled only by the range of intense emotions the piece evoked.
etymology: French painter’s boards lent metaphor to any spread of available tones.

exampleSentence: The doctor trusted that the new medication would palliate her patient’s discomfort.
etymology: Latin *pallium* cloaked severity—symptoms eased even when causes linger.

exampleSentence: Dr. Van Helsing feared that Lucy’s pallid complexion was due to an unexplained loss of blood.
etymology: Latin *pallidus* drained color as if fear bleached the cheeks.

definition: A remedy for all ills or difficulties
exampleSentence: Doctors wish there was a single panacea for every disease, but sadly there is not.
etymology: Greek *pan-* plus *akeisthai* promised heal-all drafts—fantasy tonic for every ache.

definition: An example that is a perfect pattern or model
exampleSentence: Because the new SUV was so popular, it became the paradigm upon which all others were modeled.
etymology: Greek *parádeigma* exhibited patterns others copy deliberately.

definition: An apparently contradictory statement that is perhaps true
exampleSentence: The diplomat refused to acknowledge the paradox that negotiating a peace treaty would demand more resources than waging war.
etymology: Greek roots describe opinions bucking plausible expectation—not mere puzzles.

definition: A model of excellence or perfection
exampleSentence: The mythical Helen of Troy was considered a paragon of female beauty.
etymology: Old French *paragon* measured gems on touchstones until one surpassed every rival held beside it.

exampleSentence: It was paramount that the bomb squad disconnect the blue wire before removing the fuse.
etymology: Anglo-Norman *paramont* perched authority at the height above subordinates.

exampleSentence: Following the discovery of his plagiarism, Professor Hurley was made a pariah in all academic circles.
etymology: Tamil *paṟaiyan* named drum-beaters made social untouchables—metaphor widened.

exampleSentence: A hush fell over the classroom when the teacher returned to find Deborah acting out a parody of his teaching style.
etymology: Greek *parā-* plus *ōidḗ* twisted songs into mocking echoes of originals.

exampleSentence: Many relatives believed that my aunt’s wealth resulted from her parsimony.
etymology: Latin *parsus* pinched every coin until generosity starved.

exampleSentence: The king did not believe that his rival could round up enough partisans to overthrow the monarchy.
etymology: Italian *partigiano* sharpened edges when loyalty outran evidence.

exampleSentence: The reason for Jim’s abdominal pain was made patent after the doctor performed a sonogram.
etymology: Latin *patēre* means ‘lie open,’ so truth lies exposed beyond dispute.

exampleSentence: Dr. Hastings had difficulty identifying the precise nature of Brian’s pathology.
etymology: Greek *pathos* plus *logos* names the disciplined study of what injuries bodies or institutions.

exampleSentence: Martha filled with pathos upon discovering the scrawny, shivering kitten at her door.
etymology: *Páthos* names suffering leveraged onstage—or in rhetoric—to wring humane response.

exampleSentence: Gilbert lamented the paucity of twentieth century literature courses available at the college.
etymology: Latin *paucus*, few, measures supplies too thin for comfort.

exampleSentence: The evening’s headline news covered an international scandal caused by a pejorative statement the famous senator had made in reference to a foreign leader.
etymology: Latin *peior* (‘worse’) tilts vocabulary toward downgrade and insult.

exampleSentence: Wishing his book to be pellucid to the common man, Albert Camus avoided using complicated grammar when composing The Stranger.
etymology: *Per-* intensifies *lūcēre*: prose so lucent readers never grope blindly.

exampleSentence: Jill’s dinner parties quickly became monotonous on account of her penchant for Mexican dishes.
etymology: French roots picture hanging weight tipped toward habitual liking.

exampleSentence: The jury’s verdict may have been more lenient if the criminal had appeared penitent for his gruesome crimes.
etymology: Latin *paenitēre* weighs guilt sincerely enough to spur amendment.

exampleSentence: Having smoked the penultimate cigarette remaining in the pack, Cybil discarded the last cigarette and resolved to quit smoking.
etymology: *Paene* (‘almost’) with *ultimus* docks one step shy of finale.

exampleSentence: Stella complained that her husband’s penurious ways made it impossible to live the lifestyle she felt she deserved.
etymology: *Pēnia* tethered scarcity of coin—bodily want or stingy instincts alike.

exampleSentence: After the official was caught selling government secrets to enemy agents, he was executed for his perfidious ways.
etymology: Latin *perfidia* violates sworn faith with knife-twist betrayal.

exampleSentence: The radio broadcaster announced the news of the massacre in a surprisingly perfunctory manner.
etymology: *Per-* plus *fungī* marched through chores without spirited care.

exampleSentence: Mrs. Huxtable was annoyed that the wet dog’s odor had permeated the furniture’s upholstery.
etymology: Latin *per meāre* lets liquids—or ideas—seep wholly through porous matter.

exampleSentence: The new government feared that the Communist sympathizers would have a pernicious influence on the nation’s stability.
etymology: *Per-* plus *necāre* names ruin that wastes strength quietly.

exampleSentence: Brad was perplexed by his girlfriend’s suddenly distant manner.
etymology: Latin roots tangle strands until orderly sense trips over itself.

exampleSentence: The detective was too humble to acknowledge that his perspicacity was the reason for his professional success.
etymology: Latin *perspicācitas* builds on *perspicēre* (‘see through’) for minds that sift signal from camouflage.

exampleSentence: My parents forgave Sandra’s pert humor at the dinner table because it had been so long since they had last seen her.
etymology: Older *pert* leaned on Latin *īmpetus* (‘push’) for sass that elbows past etiquette’s restraint.

exampleSentence: Harry’s parents were frustrated with his pertinacious insistence that a monster lived in his closet. Then they opened the closet door and were eaten.
etymology: *Pertināx* keeps its grip even when coaxing waits for exhaustion to loosen it first.
