exampleSentence: The actor agreed to accept the role after a two-month perusal of the movie script.
etymology: Anglo-French habits of careful paging treat close reading itself as disciplined judgment work.

definition: Having the tendency to spread throughout
exampleSentence: Stepping off the plane in Havana, I recognized the pervasive odor of sugar cane fields on fire.
etymology: *Pervādere* marched influence through porous corners of life until little stayed untouched.

exampleSentence: The Nanny resigned after she could no longer tolerate the child’s petulance.
etymology: *Petulus* tagged forward moods that snag on petty denials like burrs.

exampleSentence: Many people felt that the billionaire’s decision to donate her fortune to house the homeless was the ultimate philanthropic act.
etymology: Greek *phílos* plus *ánthrōpos* widened fondness into charity done visibly for strangers’ welfare.

exampleSentence: Monique feared her dog was ill after the animal’s phlegmatic response to his favorite chew toy.
etymology: Greek *phlégma* named sluggish humors old physiology linked to unruffled—even sleepy—steady moods.

definition: To seize or plunder, especially in war
exampleSentence: Invading enemy soldiers pillaged the homes scattered along the country’s border.
etymology: Raids stripping conquered settlements braided violence with systematic hauling away of movable wealth.

exampleSentence: Book reviewers declared that the author’s new novel was extraordinary and probably the pinnacle of Western literature.
etymology: Latin *pīna*, a spike or pin, crowned literal peaks and metaphorical crests alike.

exampleSentence: My father’s long-winded explanation was a stark contrast to his usually pithy statements.
etymology: Old English *pitha* named marrow gnawed from bone—speech pared marrow-clean of needless fat.

definition: A very small amount, especially relating to money
exampleSentence: Josh complained that he was paid a pittance for the great amount of work he did at the firm.
etymology: French *pitance* began humble charity crumbs; wages can still shrink to painfully thin driblets.

definition: To ease the anger of, soothe
exampleSentence: The man purchased a lollipop to placate his irritable son.
etymology: Latin *placāre* smooths beasts, crowds, or gods toward soothed acceptance.

exampleSentence: The placid lake surface was as smooth as glass.
etymology: *Placidus* pictured mirror-steady water echoed by moods equally unstirred.

exampleSentence: After reading over her paper, Helene concluded that what she thought were profound insights were actually just platitudes.
etymology: *Plātus* suggests flat stamping—ideas interchangeable as bland metal blanks lacking contour.

exampleSentence: The controversial new film received plaudits from even the harshest critics.
etymology: Latin *plaudere* rallied crowds honoring feats with public hand-thunder down the street.

exampleSentence: He studied all the data and then came up with a plausible theory that took all factors into account.
etymology: Latin applause cues hint what earns brief assent—even when sharper audit waits backstage.

exampleSentence: My grandmother was overwhelmed by the plenitude of tomatoes her garden yielded this season.
etymology: *Plētūdō* measures brimful fullness where scarcity cannot find footing.

exampleSentence: The wedding banquet included a plethora of oysters piled almost three feet high.
etymology: Medical Greek *plethṓrē* warned blood—or rhetoric—flooded perilously beyond healthy balance.

exampleSentence: Aircraft wings are designed to be somewhat pliable so they do not break in heavy turbulence.
etymology: French *plier* bends yielding material—or temperament—with elastic give.

exampleSentence: My teacher actually cried after reading to us the poignant final chapter of the novel.
etymology: Latin *pungere*, prick, lengthened sweet pain memory recognizes cleanly.

definition: An aggressive argument against a specific opinion
exampleSentence: My brother launched into a polemic against my arguments that capitalism was an unjust economic system.
etymology: Greek *pólemos*, war, armed rhetoric as argumentative campaigning rather than joint inquiry.

exampleSentence: When a black cat crossed my sister’s path while she was walking to school, she took it as a portent that she would do badly on her spelling test.
etymology: Latin *portentum* ‘sign, omen,’ from *portendere* ‘to stretch forth as a warning,’ from *por-* (variant of *prae-*) + *tendere* ‘to stretch.’

exampleSentence: During sea voyages it is essential that ships carry a supply of potable water because salty ocean water makes anyone who drinks it sick.
etymology: Latin *potāre*, drink, judged liquids fit—or harmless—for human throats.

definition: One who has great power, a ruler
exampleSentence: All the villagers stood along the town’s main road to observe as the potentate’s procession headed towards the capital.
etymology: *Potēns*, mighty, backed rulers commanding outsized fiat over subjects.

exampleSentence: The politician argued that while increased security measures might not fit with the lofty ideals of the nation, they were a pragmatic necessity to ensure everyone’s safety.
etymology: Greek *prāgmatikós* tethered ideals to workable deeds outsiders can inspect.

definition: The face of a cliff, a steep or overhanging place
exampleSentence: The mountain climber hung from a precipice before finding a handhold and pulling himself up.
etymology: *Praeceps*, headlong, aligned dizzy literal lips with perilous metaphorical thresholds.

exampleSentence: My grandfather’s large and vicious guard dog precluded anyone from entering the yard.
etymology: *Praecludere* shut doors preemptively on routes dissent might wander.

exampleSentence: Derek was so academically precocious that by the time he was 10 years old, he was already in the ninth grade.
etymology: *Praecox*, ripening early, praises—or worries—skills ahead of birthdays.

definition: A preference or inclination for something
exampleSentence: Francois has a predilection for eating scrambled eggs with ketchup, though I prefer to eat eggs without any condiments.
etymology: French *prédilection*, from Latin *praediligere* ‘to prefer,’ from *prae-* ‘before’ + *diligere* ‘to esteem, choose.’

exampleSentence: Britain’s preponderance of naval might secured the nation’s role as a military power.
etymology: *Praeponderāre* sank scales under one decisive mass rivals could not outweigh.

definition: Occupying the mind to the exclusion of other thoughts or feelings
exampleSentence: His prepossessing appearance made it impossible for me to think of anything else.
etymology: From *pre-* + *possess*; originally ‘occupy the mind beforehand,’ hence ‘impress favorably at first meeting.’

exampleSentence: When my uncle’s old war injury ached, he interpreted it as a presage of bad weather approaching.
etymology: French *presage*, from Latin *praesagium* ‘foreboding,’ from *praesagire* ‘to forebode,’ from *prae-* ‘before’ + *sagire* ‘to perceive keenly’ (cf. *sagax* ‘keen’).

exampleSentence: Questioning the fortune cookie’s prediction, Ray went in search of the old hermit who was rumored to be prescient.
etymology: Latin *praescientia* or adjective *praesciens*, from *praescire* ‘to know beforehand,’ from *prae-* ‘before’ + *scire* ‘to know.’

exampleSentence: The duke prescribed that from this point further all of the peasants living on his lands would have to pay higher taxes.
etymology: *Praescribere* inked authoritative rules—from judges or physicians—for others to follow.

exampleSentence: The princess grew angry after the presumptuous noble tried to kiss her, even though he was far below her in social status.
etymology: *Praesūmere*, take beforehand, leapt claims resting on swagger before proofs earned footing.

definition: An appearance or action intended to deceive
exampleSentence: Though he actually wanted to use his parents’ car to go on a date, Nick borrowed his parents’ car under the pretense of attending a group study session.
etymology: *Praetendere* stretched cover—fabric or manners—between raw truth and public face.

exampleSentence: The first primates to walk on two legs, called Australopithecus, were the primeval descendants of modern man.
etymology: Latin *primaevus* ‘of the first age,’ from *primus* ‘first’ + *aevum* ‘age’—belonging to earliest times.

exampleSentence: After decades of rule by an oppressive government that saw nothing wrong with stealing from its citizens, the recent drought only increased the people’s privation.
etymology: *Privāre* peeled essentials away until gnawing scarcity surfaced in daily living.

exampleSentence: Because he was never viewed as a man of great probity, no one was surprised by Mr. Samson’s immoral behavior.
etymology: *Probus*, upright, resisted backstage deals rumor could corrode.

exampleSentence: In a sick twist of fate, Harold’s childhood proclivity for torturing small animals grew into a desire to become a surgeon.
etymology: Latin *proclivitas* ‘a sloping forward, tendency,’ from *proclivis* ‘sloping forward,’ from *pro-* ‘forward’ + *clivus* ‘slope.’

exampleSentence: The FBI was unable to procure sufficient evidence to charge the gangster with racketeering.
etymology: *Pro-* with *curāre* marched care until needed goods landed in hand.

exampleSentence: Jacob’s profane act of dumping frogs in the holy water in the chapel at his boarding school resulted in his dismissal.
etymology: *Profānum* barred uninitiated footing around consecrated precincts—and speech that cheapens sacred things.

exampleSentence: The profligate gambler loved to drink, spend money, steal, cheat, and hang out with prostitutes.
etymology: *Pro-* plus flinging verbs hurled fortunes or reputations away until ruins showed publicly.

exampleSentence: The fans were profuse in their cheers for the star basketball player.
etymology: *Profundere* poured blossoms, thanks, or words outward without stingy counting.

exampleSentence: The film professor promulgated that both in terms of sex appeal and political intrigue, Sean Connery’s James Bond was superior to Roger Moore’s.
etymology: *Promulgāre* marched edicts into common hearing so ignorance could scarcely plead innocence.

exampleSentence: Rumors of Paul McCartney’s demise propagated like wildfire throughout the world.
etymology: Latin *propagāre*, set slips to root, marched ideas—or lineages—outward across new ground.

exampleSentence: Dermit has a propensity for dangerous activities such as bungee jumping.
etymology: *Propendēre*, hang toward something, etched reliable habits tipped one direction.

exampleSentence: The dark storm clouds visible on the horizon suggested that the weather would not be propitious for sailing.
etymology: Omens counted *propitius* leaned kindly—fortune seeming to cooperate with earnest effort.

definition: The quality or state of being proper, decent
exampleSentence: Erma’s old-fashioned parents believed that her mini-skirt lacked the propriety expected of a “nice” girl.
etymology: French *propriété* tightened conduct communities reward as fitting and polite.

exampleSentence: Heather’s prosaic recital of the poem bored the audience.
etymology: *Prōsa* named plain marching speech instead of verse’s ornament.

exampleSentence: The town council voted to proscribe the sale of alcohol on weekends.
etymology: *Prōscrībere* nailed names up publicly—to exile foes or outlaw trade legibly.

definition: Able to change shape; displaying great variety
exampleSentence: Among Nigel’s protean talents was his ability to touch the tip of his nose with his tongue.
etymology: Proteus-shaped myth lent nimble versatility—many masks, one crafty knack.

exampleSentence: The musician had never taken a guitar lesson in his life, making his prowess with the instrument even more incredible.
etymology: Old French *prouesse* crowned deeds whose skill or daring auditors retold.

exampleSentence: After losing a fortune in a stock market crash, my father vowed to practice greater prudence in future investments.
etymology: *Prūdēns*, knowing ahead, spared rash burns by tallying fallout before leaping.

definition: Eliciting or possessing an extraordinary interest in sex
exampleSentence: David’s mother was shocked by the discovery of prurient reading material hidden beneath her son’s mattress.
etymology: *Prūrīre* itched morally—eyes craving illicit detail coyly disguised as curiosity.

exampleSentence: The judge demanded order after the lawyer’s puerile attempt to object by stomping his feet on the courtroom floor.
etymology: *Puer*, boy, tinted jokes—or logic—playground-bound despite calendars.

exampleSentence: Aaron’s pugnacious nature led him to start several barroom brawls each month.
etymology: *Pugnāre*, fight, tightened reflexes awake before diplomacy cooled insults.

exampleSentence: Several of Shakespeare’s sonnets explore the pulchritude of a lovely young man.
etymology: *Pulcher*, fair or handsome, praises visible charms the public gaze notices.

definition: Eager to follow rules or conventions
exampleSentence: Punctilious Bobby, hall monitor extraordinaire, insisted that his peers follow the rules.
etymology: *Punctum*, point, tracked ceremony down to dotted *i*s without mercy.

definition: Having a pointed, sharp quality—often used to describe smells
exampleSentence: The pungent odor in the classroom made Joseph lose his concentration during the test.
etymology: *Pungere*, prick, spiced odors or sarcasm into edges refusing blur.

exampleSentence: If caught smoking in the boys’ room, the punitive result is immediate expulsion from school.
etymology: *Pūnīre* loaded judgments—courtroom or parental—with deliberate sting.

exampleSentence: Those rotten eggs smell putrid.
etymology: *Putēre*, stink, named organic betrayal noses recognize before calendars admit spoilage.

exampleSentence: We’d all like to avoid the kind of military quagmire characterized by the Vietnam War.
etymology: Quaking mire swallowed boots—plans likewise bog down with sucking inertia.

exampleSentence: Hilda was delighted by the quaint bonnets she saw in Amish country.
etymology: English gathered crafty-knowing senses into amiably antiquated charms that feel studiedly darling.

exampleSentence: Carlos found himself in a quandary: should he choose mint chocolate chip or cookie dough?
etymology: English (16th c.), origin uncertain; often linked to Latin *quando* ‘when,’ suggesting inability to decide *when* or how to choose among options.

definition: To control or diffuse a potentially explosive situation
exampleSentence: The skilled leader deftly quelled the rebellion.
etymology: Old English *cwellan* killed outright; gentler English still crushes tumults flat.

exampleSentence: If deprived of his pacifier, young Brendan becomes querulous.
etymology: *Querī*, complain, lengthened chronic grumble pitched at petty irritants.

exampleSentence: Edward entertained a quixotic desire to fall in love at first sight in a laundromat.
etymology: Cervantes’ knight lent noble fantasy lunging at problems windmills shrug off.

exampleSentence: Ambika’s quotidian routines include drinking two cups of coffee in the morning.
etymology: *Quōtīdiānus* tallied mornings stacked until spectacle thinned to numb habit.

exampleSentence: The professor railed against the injustice of the college’s tenure policy.
etymology: Middle English *railen* vented bitterness in prolonged, accusing speech shouted like hammered planks.

definition: Having a terrible taste or smell
exampleSentence: Rob was double-dog-dared to eat the rancid egg salad sandwich.
etymology: *Rancēre*, rank stench, spoiled oils—or alliances—past safe savor.

exampleSentence: When Eileen challenged me to a fight, I could see the rancor in her eyes.
etymology: Latin bitterness aged slights into poison lingering after triggers cooled.

exampleSentence: When Margaret met her paramour, they felt an instant rapport.
etymology: French *rapporter*, carry back, caught mirrored ease flowing both directions.

exampleSentence: It’s best to think things over calmly and thoroughly, rather than make rash decisions.
etymology: Middle English *rash* ‘hasty, quick,’ perhaps akin to Old Norse *röskr* ‘bold, active’; the sense ‘reckless’ develops from undue speed.

exampleSentence: Sarah’s neighbors called the cops when her house party got too raucous.
etymology: *Raucus*, hoarse, widened ears assaulted by unmodulated peals.

exampleSentence: The old tenement house was razed to make room for the large chain store.
etymology: Old French *raser*, scrape smooth, left dust heaps where masonry once bristled.

exampleSentence: When the cops showed up at Sarah’s party, they rebuked her for disturbing the peace.
etymology: Old French *rebuchier*, thrust back, hurled corrective heat straight at faults exposed.

exampleSentence: Even when scolded, the recalcitrant young girl simply stomped her foot and refused to finish her lima beans.
etymology: *Calcitrāre*, kick back, resisted orders visibly with heels dug in.

exampleSentence: Before the final exam, the teacher recapitulated the semester’s material.
etymology: *Caput*, head, marched argument vertebrae retraced cleanly top-down.

exampleSentence: When Steve gave Samantha a sweater for Christmas, she reciprocated by giving him a kiss.
etymology: *Reciprocus*, motion both ways, mirrored favors—or barbs—without lopsided ledgers.

exampleSentence: Reclusive authors such as J.D. Salinger do not relish media attention and sometimes even enjoy holing up in remote cabins in the woods.
etymology: *Claudere*, shut, lengthened solitude behind doors politely closed.
*Conciliāre* reheated goodwill once ruptures cooled toward workable armistices.

senses.exampleSentence: The feuding neighbors finally reconciled when one brought the other a delicious tuna noodle casserole.

senses.definition: To make consistent with existing ideas
senses.exampleSentence: Alou had to reconcile his skepticism about the existence of aliens with the fact that he was looking at a flying saucer.

exampleSentence: The priest’s rectitude gave him the moral authority to counsel his parishioners.
etymology: *Rectus*, straight, praised spines refusing crooked bends in duty.
French roots of *redouter*, dread anew, crowned reputations foes hesitated to poke.

senses.exampleSentence: The fortress looked redoubtable set against a stormy sky.
The audience greeted the redoubtable speaker with a standing ovation.

exampleSentence: The light was refracted as it passed through the prism.
etymology: *Frangere*, break—here light kinks obeying thresholds between media.

exampleSentence: The dingy old chair, after being refurbished, commanded the handsome price of $200.
etymology: *Furbir*, polish, marched tired interiors toward freshened gleam methodically.

exampleSentence: Maria refuted the president’s argument as she yelled and gesticulated at the TV.
etymology: *Refūtāre*, beat back, collapsed claims evidence refused sustaining.
*Gurges*, whirlpool, pictured stomachs cancelling southbound visas mechanically.

senses.exampleSentence: Feeling sick, Chuck regurgitated his dinner.
Margaret rushed through the test, regurgitating all of the facts she’d memorized an hour earlier.

etymology: *Relegāre*, send away quietly, parked people—or topics—on subsidiary tables.

senses.definition: To assign to the proper place
senses.exampleSentence: At the astrology conference, Simon was relegated to the Scorpio room.

senses.definition: To assign to an inferior place
senses.exampleSentence: After spilling a drink on a customer’s shirt, the waiter found himself relegated to the least lucrative shift.

exampleSentence: Pete always relished his bedtime snack.
etymology: Old French *releschier*, lick again, tied keen pleasure on the tongue—or in a contest—doubled willingly.

definition: Intended to repair gaps in students’ basic knowledge
exampleSentence: After his teacher discovered he couldn’t read, Alex was forced to enroll in remedial English.
etymology: *Remedium* rehealed cracks ordinary pacing skimmed past carelessly.

exampleSentence: The burglar gained entrance because the security guard, remiss in his duties, forgot to lock the door.
etymology: *Remittere* flagged duties dropped slackly instead of carried through.
*Novāre*, renew, marched studs, conduit, cladding toward code-worthy soundness.

senses.exampleSentence: The renovated antique candelabra looked as good as new.

senses.definition: To enlarge and make prettier, especially a house
senses.exampleSentence: After getting renovated, the house was twice as big and much more attractive.

exampleSentence: The young writer earned international renown by winning the Pulitzer Prize.
etymology: *Renomer*, name again, amplified reputations echoing willingly in public ear.

exampleSentence: Fiona’s renunciation of red meat resulted in weight loss, but confused those people who thought she’d been a vegetarian for years.
etymology: *Renūntiāre*, announce backward, relinquished titles or pleasures aloud without clawbacks.

exampleSentence: The repentant Dennis apologized profusely for breaking his mother’s vase.
etymology: *Paenitēre* dragged conscience toward apologies felt—not merely staged.

exampleSentence: The unedited version was replete with naughty words.
etymology: *Plēre*, fill, plus *re-* emphasized pantries—or prose—crowded past easy slack.

exampleSentence: The cat, after eating an entire can of tuna fish, reposed in the sun and took a long nap.
etymology: *Repausāre*, pause again, banked stillness limbs finally spared.

exampleSentence: Jean’s cruel and reprehensible attempt to dump her boyfriend on his birthday led to tears and recriminations.
etymology: *Reprehendere*, seize back fault, tagged conduct auditors could rebuke plainly.

exampleSentence: Because the governor woke up in a particularly good mood, he granted hundreds of reprieves to prisoners.
etymology: Anglo-French respite habits stalled execution’s blade just long enough to draw another breath.

exampleSentence: Brian reproached the customer for failing to rewind the video he had rented.
etymology: Old French *reprocher* thrust fault intimately close—the burn of visible blame lingering.

exampleSentence: The reprobate criminal sat sneering in the cell.
etymology: Latin *reprobare* (‘test and reject’) labeled persons judged unworthy of honor.

exampleSentence: Lara reproved her son for sticking each and every one of his fingers into the strawberry pie.
etymology: Latin *reprobare* sharpened into speech that faults conduct without indulgence.

exampleSentence: Kwame made a strong case for an extension of his curfew, but his mother repudiated it with a few biting words.
etymology: Latin *repudiare* refused a spouse or debt; English widened the divorce to ideas.
Latin *repellere* (‘push back’) describes shields and armies forcing an advance away.

senses.exampleSentence: Antisocial Annie tried to repulse people by neglecting to brush her teeth.
With a deft movement of her wrist and a punch to the stomach, Lacy repulsed Jack’s attempt to kiss her.

exampleSentence: After the most reputable critic in the industry gave the novel a glowing review, sales took off.
etymology: Latin *reputare* (‘count, reckon’) builds the reckoning we call a fair name.

definition: A demand for goods, usually made by an authority
exampleSentence: During the war, the government made a requisition of supplies.
etymology: Latin *requisitio* claims stores or people by rule when need is certified.

exampleSentence: The company rescinded its offer of employment after discovering that Jane’s resume was full of lies.
etymology: Latin *rescindere* (‘cut off’) cancels orders as if severing a rope.
French *réservoir* keeps captured water—literally something that *reserves* liquid.

senses.exampleSentence: Igor the Indomitable had quite a reservoir of strengh and could lift ten tons, even after running 700 miles, jumping over three mountains, and swimming across an ocean.

senses.definition: A body of water used for storing water
senses.exampleSentence: After graduation, the more rebellious members of the senior class jumped into the town reservoir used for drinking water.

definition: Able to recover from misfortune; able to withstand adversity
exampleSentence: The resilient ballplayer quickly recovered from his wrist injury.
etymology: Latin *resilire* (‘spring back’) praises materials—and people—that rebound after stress.

exampleSentence: With a resolute glint in her eye, Catherine announced that she was set on going to college in New York City even though she was a little frightened of tall buildings.
etymology: Latin *resolvere* binds one sense ‘loosen’ and another ‘set firm,’ here the latter.
Noun *resolve* is will gathered tight enough to hold a costly course.

senses.exampleSentence: Sarah and Emma resolved their differences and shook hands.
Lady Macbeth resolved to whip her husband into shape.

exampleSentence: Justin left the pub to gain a brief respite from the smoke and noise.
etymology: Anglo-French *respit* granted delays; English keeps the merciful pause.

exampleSentence: The partygoers were resplendent in diamonds and fancy dress.
etymology: Latin *resplendere* (‘shine forth’) lights cliffs, cloth, or virtue with outer glow.

exampleSentence: Many people feel that descendants of slaves should receive restitution for the sufferings of their ancestors.
etymology: Latin *restituere* sets stolen goods—or justice—back upon their proper shelf.

exampleSentence: The restive audience pelted the band with mud and yelled nasty comments.
etymology: Latin *restare* (‘stand still’) ironically names crowds impatient under restraint.

exampleSentence: As the media worked itself into a frenzy, the publicist hurriedly retracted his client’s sexist statement.
etymology: Latin *retractare* handles a claim again until it is pulled inside.

exampleSentence: Theodore reveled in his new status as Big Man on Campus.
etymology: Old French *reveler* tied noisy festivity to disorder that feels like harmless riot.

exampleSentence: The doctor saved countless lives with his combination of expertise and kindness and became universally revered.
etymology: Latin *revereri* (‘fear in awe’) lowers voices before grave excellence.

exampleSentence: After missing the curfew set by the court for eight nights in a row, Marcel’s freedom of movement was revoked.
etymology: Latin *revocare* (‘call back’) withdraws permission as a herald might recall a decree.

exampleSentence: The critic rhapsodized about the movie, calling it an instant classic.
etymology: Greek *rhapsōidía* stitched song; English verbs lift speech toward lyric height.

exampleSentence: While some giggled at the ribald joke involving a parson’s daughter, most sighed and rolled their eyes.
etymology: Old French *ribaud* named wandering soldiers whose jokes stayed loud and coarse.

exampleSentence: Surprisingly, the famous novelist’s writing was rife with spelling errors.
etymology: Old English *rīfe* meant copious growth; rumor and error may run equally *rife*.

exampleSentence: Terry liked to ruminate while sitting on the banks of the river, staring pensively into the water.
etymology: Latin *ruminari* (‘chew cud’) borrowed the cow’s patience for slow thought.

exampleSentence: Oliver concocted an elaborate ruse for sneaking out of the house to meet his girlfriend while simultaneously giving his mother the impression that he was asleep in bed.
etymology: Old French *ruser* (‘dodge’) folded feints into plans that sidestep straight fight.

exampleSentence: Tom’s saccharine manner, although intended to make him popular, actually repelled his classmates.
etymology: Latin *saccharum* (‘sugar’) sugared tone until listeners feel their teeth ache.

definition: Holy, something that should not be criticized
exampleSentence: In the United States, the Constitution is often thought of as a sacrosanct document.
etymology: Latin *sacer* (‘sacred’) plus *sanctus* marks zones mockery must not enter.

exampleSentence: With remarkable sagacity, the wise old man predicted and thwarted his children’s plan to ship him off to a nursing home.
etymology: Latin *sagax* (‘keen of scent’) praised trackers—then judges—who notice slightest traces.

exampleSentence: One of the salient differences between Alison and Nancy is that Alison is a foot taller.
etymology: Latin *salīre* (‘leap’) makes the fact that jumps before quieter details.

exampleSentence: Andrew regularly began letters with the bizarre salutation “Ahoy ahoy.”
etymology: Latin *salūtātiō* from *salūs* (‘health, safety’) begins speech with goodwill’s bow.

exampleSentence: After Tony applied a salve to his brilliant red sunburn, he soon felt a little better.
etymology: Old English *sealf* and Latin *salvum* met in soothing salves for skin and shame.

definition: Giving a hypocritical appearance of piety
exampleSentence: The sanctimonious Bertrand delivered stern lectures on the Ten Commandments to anyone who would listen, but thought nothing of stealing cars to make some cash on the side.
etymology: Latin *sanctimonia* feigned holiness loudly worn for show.

exampleSentence: Polly reacted to any bad news with a sanguine smile and the chirpy cry, “When life hands you lemons, make lemonade!”
etymology: Latin *sanguis* (‘blood’) flushed healthy confidence into European medical metaphor.

exampleSentence: Satiated after eating far too much turkey and stuffing, Liza lay on the couch watching football and suffering from stomach pains.
etymology: Latin *satiare* packs the belly—or audience—past any polite stopping point.

exampleSentence: Two hours after breaking up with Russell, Suzanne thought of the perfect scathing retort to his accusations.
etymology: Old Norse *skaða* (‘hurt’) became English *scathe*, then adjective *scathing*.

exampleSentence: The ice skater’s scintillating rhinestone costume nearly blinded the judges.
etymology: Latin *scintilla* (‘spark’) leaps from steel, stars, and rapid minds alike.

exampleSentence: With scrupulouscare, Sam cut a snowflake out of white paper.
etymology: Latin *scrupulus* (‘small sharp stone’) stood for pebbles troubling conscience’s shoe.

exampleSentence: When Bruno heard the scurrilous accusation being made about him, he could not believe it because he always tried to be nice to everyone.
etymology: Latin *scurrilis* (‘buffoonish’) from *scurra* (‘city idler’) sank speech into mud.

exampleSentence: The sedentary cat did little but loll in the sun.
etymology: Latin *sedēre* (‘sit’) marks lives that seldom rise to stretch or roam.

exampleSentence: Anne and Diana communicated with a semaphore involving candles and window shades.
etymology: Greek *sēma* (‘sign’) and *phorein* (‘carry’) named cloth alphabets waved at sea.

exampleSentence: Stephen Greenblatt’s essays on Shakespeare proved to be seminal, because they initiated the critical school of New Historicism.
etymology: Latin *sēmen* (‘seed’) scatters papers that sprout whole schools after.

definition: Involving sensory gratification, usually related to sex
exampleSentence: With a coy smile, the guest on the blind-date show announced that he considered himself a very sensual person.
etymology: Latin *sēnsus* narrows to touch, taste, and appetite’s warmer claims.

exampleSentence: Paul found drinking Coke, with all the little bubbles bursting on his tongue, a very sensuous experience.
etymology: Latin *sēnsus* widens to aesthetic touch—velvet light, slow music, ripe color.

definition: Luck, finding good things without looking for them
exampleSentence: In an amazing bit of serendipity, penniless Paula found a $20 bill in the subway station.
etymology: The tale of three princes of *Serendip* made accident look like wit.

exampleSentence: Louise stood in front of the Mona Lisa, puzzling over the famous woman’s serene smile.
etymology: Latin *serēnus* cleared storm from sky and temper alike.

exampleSentence: The servile porter crept around the hotel lobby, bowing and quaking before the guests.
etymology: Latin *servilis* bent the knee low before whoever held the whip.

exampleSentence: With the sinuous movements of her arms, the dancer mimicked the motion of a snake.
etymology: Latin *sinuare* (‘bend in folds’) follows oxbow rivers and sinuous prose.

exampleSentence: Jason believed that maintaining his sobriety in times of crisis was the key to success in life.
etymology: Latin *sōbrius* began ‘not drunk’ and rose toward steady judgment.

exampleSentence: Jim, laid up in bed with a nasty virus, enjoyed the solicitous attentions of his mother, who brought him soup and extra blankets.
etymology: Latin *sollicitus* (‘thoroughly stirred’) worries over another’s welfare with care.

definition: Believing that oneself is all that exists
exampleSentence: Colette’s solipsistic attitude completely ignored the plight of the homeless people on the street.
etymology: Latin *solus* (‘alone’) with *ipse* (‘self’) traps theory in one private theater.

exampleSentence: The plot of the spy film revolved around an untraceable and water-soluble poison.
etymology: Latin *solvere* (‘loosen’) marks salts that surrender to water’s embrace.
Chemistry’s *solvent* is the liquid that loosens solute into solution.

senses.definition: A substance that can dissolve other substances
senses.exampleSentence: Water is sometimes called the universal solvent because almost all other substances can dissolve into it.
Upon receiving an unexpected check from her aunt, Annabelle found herself suddenly solvent.

exampleSentence: The somnolent student kept falling asleep and waking up with a jerk.
etymology: Latin *somnus* (‘sleep’) thickened eyelids toward half-dream.

exampleSentence: The mature senior rolled her eyes at the sophomoric gross-out humor of the underclassman.
etymology: Greek *sophos* plus *mōros* (‘wise fool’) tags the sophomore’s swagger.

definition: Having absolute authority in a certain realm
exampleSentence: The sovereign queen, with steely resolve, ordered that the traitorous nobleman be killed.
etymology: Old French *sovereign* crowned Latin *superānus* (‘highest’) with full command.

exampleSentence: Sadly, Tessa was convicted on merely speculative evidence.
etymology: Latin *speculārī* (‘look out’) let traders guess at cargoes still below the horizon.

definition: False but designed to seem plausible
exampleSentence: Using a spurious argument, John convinced the others that he had won the board game on a technicality.
etymology: Latin *spurius* marked illegitimate birth; English marks illegitimate claims.

definition: To become or remain inactive, not develop, not flow
exampleSentence: With no room for advancement, the waiter’s career stagnated.
etymology: Latin *stagnum* (‘standing water’) pictures pools that cease to flow.

exampleSentence: The staid butler never changed his expression no matter what happened.
etymology: Latin *stāre* in *staid* marks characters that stand steady without theatrics.

definition: Not generous, not inclined to spend or give
exampleSentence: Scrooge’s stingy habits did not fit with the generous, giving spirit of Christmas.
etymology: Old English *stingan* (‘sting’) nicked coins as if each hurt.

exampleSentence: Penelope’s faithfulness to Odysseus required that she be stoic and put off her many suitors.
etymology: Greek *Stōikos* named porch philosophers who practiced pain’s long patience.

exampleSentence: Charles’s stolid reaction to his wife’s funeral differed from the passion he showed at the time of her death.
etymology: Latin *stolidus* (‘dull’) labels minds that seldom quicken to surprise.

exampleSentence: Running a marathon is quite a strenuous task. So is watching an entire Star Trek marathon.
etymology: Latin *strenuus* (‘brisk, hardy’) praises strain willingly borne.

exampleSentence: A strident man, Captain Von Trapp yelled at his daughter and made her cry.
etymology: Latin *stridēre* (‘creak, shriek’) names noise that scrapes the ear.

exampleSentence: Veronica’s audacity and ungratefulness stupefied her best friend, Heather.
etymology: Latin *stupēre* (‘be stunned’) names the club blow to thought.

exampleSentence: The invading force captured and subjugated the natives of that place.
etymology: Latin *iugum* (‘yoke’) reappears in *subiugāre*, forcing necks under rule.

exampleSentence: The homeless man sadly pondered his former wealth and once sublime existence.
etymology: Latin *sublimis* (‘raised aloft’) lifts peak, poetry, or terror toward sky.

exampleSentence: In some cultures, wives are supposed to be submissive and support their husbands in all matters.
etymology: Latin *submittere* (‘place under’) bends will beneath another’s hand.

exampleSentence: The governor’s succinct speech energized the crowd while the mayor’s rambled on and on.
etymology: Latin *succingere* (‘gird from below’) tucked arguments tight like a belt.

exampleSentence: Tracy had already won the campaign so her constant flattery of others was superfluous.
etymology: Latin *superfluus* (‘overflowing’) spills more than use can drink.

exampleSentence: After partaking of the surfeit of tacos and tamales at the All-You-Can-Eat Taco Tamale Lunch Special, Beth felt rather sick.
etymology: Old French *surfet* filled the stomach until disgust arrived.

exampleSentence: After speaking to only one of the students, the teacher was able to surmise what had caused the fight.
etymology: Old French *surmise* overtook Latin *supermittere* for notions lightly launched.

exampleSentence: The surreptitious CIA agents were able to get in and out of the house without anyone noticing.
etymology: Latin *subripere* (‘snatch secretly’) hid hands inside sleeves.

definition: One acting in place of another
exampleSentence: The surrogate carried the child to term for its biological parents.
etymology: Latin *surrogātus* (‘asked in place of’) names proxies who wear another’s chair.

exampleSentence: When he got drunk, Robinson’s white skin became rather swarthy.
etymology: Old English *sweart* warmed into *swarthy* for deep sun-seasoned hue.

exampleSentence: Some see the people in the cabinet as the president’s closest advisors, but others see them as sycophants.
etymology: Greek *sūkon* (‘fig’) plus *phainein* whispered informers; English keeps oily praise.

exampleSentence: I interpreted my parents’ refusal to talk as a tacit acceptance of my request.
etymology: Latin *tacitus* (‘silent’) binds parties without ink.

exampleSentence: Though Jane never seems to stop talking, her brother is quite taciturn.
etymology: Latin *tacitus* with *-urnus* marks habitually quiet temperaments.

exampleSentence: I tried to discuss my salary, but the boss kept veering off into tangential topics.
etymology: Latin *tangere* (‘touch’) touches a subject only at odd edges.

exampleSentence: When it comes to sports, fearing your opponent is tantamount to losing.
etymology: Anglo-Norman *tant amunter* amounted sums until two claims matched.

exampleSentence: As time passed and the history professor continued to drone on and on, the lecture became increasingly tedious.
etymology: Latin *taedium* bored the spirit until clocks crawled.

exampleSentence: Tom and Huck entered the scary cave armed with nothing but their own temerity.
etymology: Latin *temeritas* (‘blind chance’) gambled life on dice without looking.

exampleSentence: Maintaining temperance will ensure that you are able to think rationally and objectively.
etymology: Latin *temperantia* mixed life’s cup without drowning draughts.

definition: Able to be defended or maintained
exampleSentence: The department heads tore down the arguments in other people’s theses, but Johari’s work proved to be quite tenable.
etymology: Latin *tenēre* (‘hold’) asks whether an argument can be held against siege.

exampleSentence: Your argument is very tenuous, since it relies so much on speculation and hearsay.
etymology: Latin *tenuis* stretched membranes until almost transparent.

exampleSentence: Elephants are terrestrial animals.
etymology: Latin *terra* (‘earth’) keeps feet on soil beneath airy theory.

exampleSentence: When dealing with the unknown, timorous Tallulah almost always broke into tears.
etymology: Latin *timor* (‘fear’) shrank spirits from risks others barely notice.

definition: A long speech marked by harsh or biting language
exampleSentence: Every time Jessica was late, her boyfriend went into a long tirade about punctuality.
etymology: French *tirade* named volleys of speech shot in prolonged bursts.

definition: One who flatters in the hope of gaining favors
exampleSentence: The other kids referred to the teacher’s pet as the Tenth Grade Toady.
etymology: Bird *toad* and *toddle* met insult: *toady* eats pride to curry favor.

exampleSentence: In college, I used to carry around an anatomy book that was the heaviest tome in my bag.
etymology: Greek *tomos* (‘cut, slice’) named rolls cut into codex chunks.

exampleSentence: The torpid whale floated, wallowing in the water for hours.
etymology: Latin *torpēre* (‘be numb’) froze limbs and markets alike.

exampleSentence: I didn’t want to witness the neighbor’s torrid affair through the window.
etymology: Latin *torrēre* (‘parch’) scorched sand, love, and rhetoric with heat.

exampleSentence: The scary thing about driving in mountains are the narrow, tortuous roads.
etymology: Latin *torquēre* (‘twist’) bent roads, plots, and spines into knots.

exampleSentence: The horse was so tractable, Myra didn’t even need a bridle.
etymology: Latin *tractāre* (‘handle’) names beings that yield to the rein.

exampleSentence: There is a time of night when nothing moves and everything is tranquil.
etymology: Latin *trānquillus* stilled water until reflections held steady.

definition: To violate, go over a limit
exampleSentence: The criminal’s actions transgressed morality and human decency.
etymology: Latin *trānsgredī* (‘step across’) crossed lines etched by rule or gods.

definition: Passing through briefly; passing into and out of existence
exampleSentence: Because virtually everyone in Palm Beach is a tourist, the population of the town is quite transient.
etymology: Latin *trānsīre* (‘go across’) marks guests who never unpack.

definition: To change or alter in form
exampleSentence: Ancient alchemists believed that it was possible to transmute lead into gold.
etymology: Latin *trānsmūtāre* shifted nature the way heat shifts ore.

exampleSentence: According to the school newspaper’s merciless theater critic, Pacific Coast High’s rendition of the musical Oklahoma was a travesty of the original.
etymology: French *travestir* (‘disguise’) dressed solemn things in clown clothes.

exampleSentence: I always feel a trifle tremulous when walking through a graveyard.
etymology: Latin *tremere* (‘tremble’) shook voice, hand, or leaf alike.

exampleSentence: The directions that accompanied my new cell phone were trenchant and easy to follow.
etymology: Old French *trenchier* (‘cut’) carved arguments to the bone.

exampleSentence: Feeling great trepidation, Anya refused to jump into the pool because she thought she saw a shark in it.
etymology: Latin *trepidus* (‘trembling’) edged every step with imagined pits.

exampleSentence: Keith thought of himself as being very learned, but everyone else thought he was trite because his observations about the world were always the same as David Letterman’s.
etymology: Latin *trītus* (‘worn by rubbing’) names phrases polished smooth by reuse.

exampleSentence: This club doesn’t really attract the dangerous types, so why was that bouncer being so truculent?
etymology: Latin *trux* (‘fierce, savage’) sharpened animals—and tempers.

exampleSentence: After winning the derby, the jockey truncated the long speech he had planned and thanked only his mom and his horse.
etymology: Latin *truncus* (‘maimed, trunk’) lopped tails from tales and trees.

definition: Swollen, excessively embellished in style or language
exampleSentence: The haughty writer did not realize how we all really felt about his turgid prose.
etymology: Latin *turgidus* swelled veins, clouds, and prose beyond their seams.

exampleSentence: Sir Marcus’s chivalry often contrasted with the turpitude he exhibited with the ladies at the tavern.
etymology: Latin *turpis* (‘shameful’) sank conduct beyond easy washing.

exampleSentence: It seems that everyone in the United States has a television. The technology is ubiquitous here.
etymology: Latin *ubīque* (‘anywhere’) multiplied a thing until no corner lacks it.

exampleSentence: He called me a lily-livered coward, and I took umbrage at the insult.
etymology: Latin *umbra* (‘shade’) named shadows of irritation crossing the face.

exampleSentence: Luka had an uncanny ability to know exactly what other people were thinking. She also had an uncanny ability to shoot fireballs from her hands.
etymology: Scots *uncanny* once meant ‘malicious’; modern use stresses the odd beyond reason.

definition: Smooth or greasy in texture, appearance, manner
exampleSentence: The unctuous receptionist seemed untrustworthy, as if she was only being helpful because she thought we might give her a big tip.
etymology: Latin *unguere* (‘anoint’) greased flattery until it slid too smooth.

exampleSentence: As the storm began to brew, the placid ocean began to undulate to an increasing degree.
etymology: Latin *undula* (‘little wave’) traced hills, snakes, and cloth in motion.

exampleSentence: The last thing Lindsay wanted was for Lisa to upbraid her again about missing the rent payment.
etymology: Old English *ūp* plus *breġdan* ‘plucked threads upward’ into sharp reproof.

definition: To seize by force, take possession of without right
exampleSentence: The rogue army general tried to usurp control of the government, but he failed because most of the army backed the legally elected president.
etymology: Latin *ūrpare* meant ‘use’; *usurpāre* took rights another should hold.

definition: Relating to or aiming at usefulness
exampleSentence: The beautiful, fragile vase couldn’t hold flowers or serve any other utilitarian purpose.
etymology: Latin *ūtilis* weighed choices by outcomes people can actually use.

definition: An imaginary and remote place of perfection
exampleSentence: Everyone in the world wants to live in a utopia, but no one can agree how to go about building one.
etymology: Greek *ou* (‘not’) plus *topos* (‘place’) named good islands on no real map.

exampleSentence: I prefer a definite answer, but my boss kept vacillating between the distinct options available to us.
etymology: Latin *vacillāre* (‘sway’) kept judgment rocking between two stools.

definition: Lack of content or ideas, stupid
exampleSentence: Beyonce realized that the lyrics she had just penned were completely vacuous and tried to add more substance.
etymology: Latin *vacuus* hollowed heads until echo answered inside.

exampleSentence: Yoko’s chemistry lab partner was asleep during the experiment and could not validate the accuracy of her methods.
etymology: Medieval Latin *validāre* strengthened claims until they stand trial.

exampleSentence: The professor’s comments about the poem were surprisingly vapid and dull.
etymology: Latin *vapidus* (‘flat-tasting’) described wine—and wit—gone spiritless.

exampleSentence: Each wire in the engineering exam was variegated by color so that the students could figure out which one was which.
etymology: Latin *varius* (‘varied’) with *-āre* lent English leaves and fabrics many streaked tones.

definition: Marked by intense force or emotion
exampleSentence: The candidate vehemently opposed cutting back on Social Security funding.
etymology: Latin *vehēment-* (‘carrying along’) drove adverbs toward violent conviction.

definition: A superficial or deceptively attractive appearance, façade
exampleSentence: Thanks to her Chanel makeup, Shannen was able to maintain a veneer of perfection that hid the flaws underneath.
etymology: German *Furnier* and French *fournir* gave English a skin of fine wood.

definition: Deserving of respect because of age or achievement
exampleSentence: The venerable Supreme Court justice had made several key rulings in landmark cases throughout the years.
etymology: Latin *venerābilis* applied to gray wisdom the crowd steps back to honor.

definition: To regard with respect or to honor
exampleSentence: The tribute to John Lennon sought to venerate his music, his words, and his legend.
etymology: Latin *vēnerārī* approached shrines with lowered eyes and measured speech.

exampleSentence: With several agencies regulating the reports, it was difficult for Latifah to argue against its veracity.
etymology: Latin *vērācitās* weighed words on scales that refuse polite weighting.

exampleSentence: It took the verbose teacher two hours to explain the topic, while it should have taken only fifteen minutes.
etymology: Latin *verbōsus* packed sentences with more sacks than cargo needs.

exampleSentence: The verdant leaves on the trees made the world look emerald.
etymology: Old French *vert* from Latin *viridis* painted hills newly waked.

definition: A mark or trace of something lost or vanished
exampleSentence: Do you know if the Mexican tortilla is a vestige of some form of Aztec corn-based flat bread?
etymology: Latin *vestīgium* (‘footstep’) marks where something once stood—barely.

exampleSentence: My little brother vexes me by poking me in the ribs for hours on end.
etymology: Latin *vexāre* (‘shake, jostle’) shook temper until knots formed.

exampleSentence: All of my lame friends learned to be social through vicarious involvement in my amazing experiences.
etymology: Latin *vicārius* (‘substitute’) felt another’s pain—or victory—from afar.

exampleSentence: The vicissitudes of daily life prevent me from predicting what might happen from one day to the next.
etymology: Latin *vicis* (‘change, turn’) counted fate’s revolving chair.

exampleSentence: The guards remained vigilant throughout the night, but the enemy never launched the expected attack.
etymology: Latin *vigil* (‘awake’) kept lamps trimmed while others slept.

exampleSentence: After the Watergate scandal, almost any story written about President Nixon sought to vilify him and criticize his behavior.
etymology: Latin *vilis* (‘cheap, base’) plus *-fy* dragged names through cheap mud.

definition: To avenge; to free from allegation; to set free
exampleSentence: The attorney had no chance of vindicating the defendant with all of the strong evidence presented by the state.
etymology: Latin *vindex* (‘avenger, defender’) cleared honor with evidence’s blade.

exampleSentence: The vindictive madman seeks to exact vengeance for any insult that he perceives is directed at him, no matter how small.
etymology: Latin *vindicta* (‘vengeance’) nursed grudges past prudence.

definition: One who excels in an art; a highly skilled musical performer
exampleSentence: Even though Lydia has studied piano for many years, she’s only average at it. She’s no virtuoso, that’s for sure.
etymology: Italian *virtuoso* praised skill so polished it looks like virtue.

exampleSentence: The viscous syrup took three minutes to pour out of the bottle.
etymology: Latin *viscum* (*birdlime* sticky) kin to *viscus* lent gluey drag to liquids.

exampleSentence: When angry, the woman would spew vitriolic insults.
etymology: Latin *vitrium* (‘glass’) tangled with strong acids that etch metal and tone.

exampleSentence: Jack ran away as soon as his father found out, knowing he would be vituperated for his unseemly behavior.
etymology: Latin *vituperāre* (‘blame’) hurled fault-finding like clods.

exampleSentence: The vivacious clown makes all of the children laugh and giggle with his friendly antics.
etymology: Latin *vīvere* (‘live’) stepped dances with quick feet and quicker wit.

definition: The work in which someone is employed, profession
exampleSentence: After growing tired of the superficial world of high-fashion, Edwina decided to devote herself to a new vocation: social work.
etymology: Latin *vocātiō* (‘a calling’) named work that summons like a horn.

exampleSentence: I’m tired of his vociferous whining so I’m breaking up with him.
etymology: Latin *vōx* plus *ferre* (‘carry’) bore noise to the farthest benches.

definition: To roll oneself indolently; to become or remain helpless
exampleSentence: My roommate can’t get over her breakup with her boyfriend and now just wallows in self-pity.
etymology: Old Germanic roots of *wallow* rolled beasts—and souls—in mud or pity.

exampleSentence: Don’t be so afraid of his wrath because his influence with the president is already beginning to wane.
etymology: Old English *wanian* thinned the moon—and hope—toward dark.

exampleSentence: Vicky’s wanton demeanor often made the frat guys next door very excited.
etymology: Middle English *wantowen* (‘undisciplined’) slipped into moral chaos.

exampleSentence: The whimsical little girl liked to pretend that she was an elvin princess.
etymology: From *whim*, English *whimsical* lets fancy tilt the logical chessboard.

exampleSentence: Though they were not the strongest of the Thundercats, wily Kit and Kat were definitely the most clever and full of tricks.
etymology: Old Norse *vīgr* (‘battle-wise’) shaded into sly cleverness.

exampleSentence: After such a long, frustrating day, I was grateful for Chris’s winsome attitude and childish naivete.
etymology: Old English *wynsum* mingled joy (*wyn*) with inviting faces.

exampleSentence: Since her pet rabbit died, Edda missed it terribly and sat around wistful all day long.
etymology: From an older sense tied to *wist* (‘intent gaze’), English *wistful* softened into gentle longing.

exampleSentence: Agatha’s grandmother, Stephanie, had the most wizened countenance, full of leathery wrinkles.
etymology: Older *wizened* tied to wise suggests skin drawn tight by many winters.

exampleSentence: Did you really want to incur her wrath when she is known for inflicting the worst punishments legally possible?
etymology: Old English *wrǣththu* burned hot as twisted bands of flame.

exampleSentence: We yoked together the logs by tying a string around them.
etymology: Old English *geoc* named wood that links two beasts—and metaphoric burdens.

definition: Fervent, filled with eagerness in pursuit of something
exampleSentence: If he were any more zealous about getting his promotion, he’d practically live at the office.
etymology: Greek *zēlos* (‘ardor, rivalry’) fired reformers and fans alike.

exampleSentence: I was too nice to tell Nelly that she had reached the absolute zenith of her career with that one hit of hers.
etymology: Medieval Latin *cenit* reshaped Arabic *samt* (‘path, direction’) into the point directly overhead.

exampleSentence: If not for the zephyrs that were blowing and cooling us, our room would’ve been unbearably hot.
etymology: Greek *Zephyros* named the west wind that warms shores toward spring.
